title: Bachelor in Communication Design (B.Des) Course
description: Build a creative career with Bachelor in Communication Design (B.Des) at WPU Goa, featuring practical training, industry exposure and design opportunities.

blocks.text: B.Des. in Communication Design

The B.Des. in Communication Design at WPU GŌA prepares students to engage with these questions through a contemporary, studio-driven, and transdisciplinary design education grounded in visual culture, systems thinking, storytelling, interaction, technology, and real-world communication practice.

At WPU GŌA, communication design is approached not merely as graphic design, but as a discipline that integrates typography, branding, digital media, motion systems, interaction design, animation, storytelling, interface design, strategy, culture, psychology, and emerging technologies.

Students learn how communication operates across identities, institutions, public systems, brands, digital platforms, media ecosystems, and everyday human interactions. The programme stands apart through its integration of design with technology, behavioural understanding, systems thinking, storytelling, entrepreneurship, and immersive media practice. Through intensive studios, digital labs, field immersions, collaborative projects, and interdisciplinary learning environments, students develop the ability to create communication systems that are strategic, meaningful, future-oriented, and socially responsible.

You will be taught by faculty who are practising designers, animators, strategists, visual communicators, researchers, media practitioners, and technologists connected to WPU GŌA’s Centres of Excellence and interdisciplinary ecosystems. Their industry work, research, and professional practice directly inform classroom learning, ensuring students understand not only how to create communication, but why communication shapes society.

The B.Des. in Communication Design at WPU GŌA is a four-year undergraduate degree for students who aspire to design visual identities, narratives, media systems, interaction environments, motion experiences, immersive communication platforms, and digital ecosystems that are human-centred, culturally aware, technologically informed, and strategically meaningful.

The programme develops strong foundations across typography and visual systems, branding and identity design, narrative and storytelling systems, and motion graphics and time-based media. Depending on their chosen within-domain specialisation, students also learn interaction design and interface systems, information and communication design, and animation and visual storytelling. Students are taught communication strategy and semiotics, user experience and digital interaction, and design research and cultural analysis. They become adept at emerging media, immersive communication, and human-centred communication systems.

Students begin with common design foundations shared across all B.Des. programmes before progressively moving into advanced visual systems, interaction environments, transmedia communication, animation ecosystems, digital storytelling, and specialised communication practice.

A defining strength of the programme is its dual-specialisation model. Every student develops depth through a Within-Domain Specialisation while also building interdisciplinary breadth through a Cross-Domain Specialisation.

Graduates emerge prepared for careers across branding, interaction design, visual communication, animation, motion graphics, UI/UX ecosystems, media strategy, digital storytelling, communication systems, design consulting, content ecosystems, and emerging immersive media industries.

Creative Professionals, Not Just Design Students: The programme develops agency-ready communication designers capable of conceptual thinking, strategic execution, storytelling, and professional delivery across multiple media environments.
Multi-Platform Story Ecosystems: Students design communication systems that operate across print, motion, interaction, digital platforms, environments, interfaces, and social media ecosystems.

Design for Complexity & Public Systems: Students work on communication challenges related to sustainability, governance, healthcare, public information, social impact, civic engagement, and digital literacy.

Information & Data as Visual Power: Students learn how to transform complex information into accessible visual communication systems, preparing them for roles across media, research, policy, and public communication.

Studios Every Semester: Project-driven studios every semester focused on real communication challenges, from brand systems and campaigns to information visualisation, motion storytelling, and interactive experiences. This hands-on approach builds both creative excellence and professional readiness.

Communication Across Media Futures: Students explore emerging communication forms including immersive media, AI-assisted storytelling, motion systems, interactive environments, and digital communication ecosystems.

Goa as a Living Communication Environment: Students engage with tourism, culture, public spaces, hospitality, media systems, local communities, creative industries, and evolving digital cultures unique to Goa’s globally connected environment.
Human-Centred Communication: Students learn how design influences behaviour, perception, trust, accessibility, and human interaction across digital and physical systems.

Strong Making & Digital Media Culture: Students work extensively with digital media labs, motion graphics systems, interface prototyping, visual systems, animation pipelines, storytelling tools, and interaction environments.

Entrepreneurial & Industry-Oriented Pathways: Students can extend projects into startups, creative studios, independent practices, media ventures, content ecosystems, and innovation-led communication enterprises.

Transdisciplinary & Strategic Focus: Combines visual craft with research, strategy, behavioural insight, technology, and systems thinking. Students learn to design not just beautiful visuals, but effective, ethical, and impactful communication systems that inform, persuade, and connect across cultures and platforms.

Dual Specialisation Model: One Within-Domain specialisation for deep expertise in visual identity, animation/VFX, or interaction design, plus one Cross-Domain specialisation for breadth; a defining feature that equips graduates with versatile, future-ready capabilities valued by industry.

Extensive Real-World Immersions: Global academic exposure, rural community engagement, Asian innovation trade shows, national residencies, industry internships, and civic programmes provide rich contextual understanding and industry connections.

Strong Portfolio & Professional Development: Continuous digital portfolio building, live briefs, client-style projects, and critique-driven studios help students graduate with industry-ready work and clear professional positioning.

Future-Ready Career Preparation: Excellent readiness for roles in branding, advertising, digital media, UX/UI, motion design, information design, social impact communication, publishing, and creative direction, with strong foundations for higher studies at leading global institutions.

Common Core: Every student at WPU GŌA, irrespective of their major, completes this carefully designed 40-credit University Common Core. It forms the intellectual bedrock of the entire academic ecosystem and develops ten essential graduate attributes. The Common Core equips students with transversal skills that transcend any single discipline. It creates a shared intellectual language across engineers, designers, psychologists, and managers so that graduates can collaborate effectively on complex, real-world problems. It ensures every student graduates with strong critical thinking, ethical reasoning, communication fluency, data literacy, and systemic perspective, capabilities that are increasingly valued in leadership roles.

Design Domain Foundation & Programme Core : Rigorous grounding in design fundamentals, form and aesthetics, visual systems, typography, user-centred research, digital design tools, prototyping techniques, and design strategy. It progresses logically from Year 1 to Year 3, moving from foundational visual and making skills to advanced communication systems thinking and complex project execution. The programme core builds strong disciplinary competence and functional mastery before students move into specialisations. This ensures graduates possess a solid design toolkit that is both conceptually deep and practically applicable.

Within-Domain Specialisation: Students select one specialisation track in Year 3 and complete advanced, domain-specific courses that provide significant depth. The specialisation tracks enable students to develop specialised expertise that prepares them for focused professional roles, entrepreneurship, or higher studies in their chosen area.
The three available tracks are:
(1) Visual Communication & Identity Systems Design (2) Animation and VFX Design (3) Interaction Design

Directed Electives: These advanced elective courses offer students the flexibility to deepen their expertise within the chosen Within-Domain specialisation or to explore closely related topics that align with their academic and professional interests. Examples include Advanced Typography, Design Futures & Speculative Communication, Inclusive Communication Systems, Sustainable Media Systems, AI Tools for Designers, Public Policy Communication Design, Motion Design for Branding, Data Visualisation & Information Design, and Transmedia Storytelling Strategies.

The Directed Electives allow significant customisation, enabling students to tailor their degree according to their specific career aspirations, whether in brand identity systems, motion graphics, interaction design, information visualisation, social impact communication, or emerging interdisciplinary fields.

Cross-Domain Specialisation: In addition to their primary communication design specialisation, every student completes one Cross-Domain Specialisation from another academic cluster (Engineering, Humanities, or Business).
Curated options include:
Computational Thinking & Creative Coding
Behavioural & Cognitive Psychology
Entrepreneurship in Creative Industries

Students may also design a custom cross-domain pathway under faculty guidance. This additional pathway is a defining feature of WPU GŌA graduates and a critical requirement for solving complex, real-world problems. It fosters transdisciplinary fluency and inculcates the ability to integrate communication design insight with computational thinking, human behaviour understanding, or entrepreneurial frameworks, enabling graduates to address societal challenges at the intersection of visual storytelling, technology, innovation, and business.

Capstone / Signature Work: The culminating experience of the programme is a full-semester, 12-credit Capstone Project in Semester 8. Students undertake a substantial, real-world integrative project which may be a comprehensive brand identity system, transmedia storytelling campaign, information design solution for public systems, motion-driven narrative project, interactive digital experience, or social impact communication initiative. The Capstone is mentored by a faculty–industry panel and requires students to synthesise knowledge from their Within-Domain specialisation, Cross-Domain specialisation, extensive immersions, Independent Inquiry Period (IIP), and core communication design foundations into a meaningful, professional, and portfolio-ready contribution.

Experiential & Integrative Elements: To ensure every graduate has substantial real-world exposure, reflective practice, and the ability to translate theory into impactful action, learning at WPU GŌA extends far beyond classrooms through a rich ecosystem of experiential components:
Studio-Based Learning : At least one studio every semester — intensive, project-driven experiences focused on real organisational challenges.
Independent Inquiry Period (IIP) : A four-week, credit-bearing, self-directed deep-dive after Semester 5.
Grand Challenge Studios & Transdisciplinary Projects : Collaborative work on university-wide societal challenges.
Continuous Digital Portfolio : A living record of projects, reflections, and achievements.
Mandatory Immersion s : Global, national, rural, industry, trade shows, LRP at LTC, and Student Active Journey in Administration & Governance (SAJAG) Programme (detailed in the Immersions section).
What Will You Learn?
Students begin by developing visual literacy, drawing capability, design observation, systems thinking, and communication fundamentals. They explore:
Drawing & visual communication
Design principles and composition
Typography foundations
Visual culture and meaning
Design thinking and problem framing
Storytelling fundamentals
Mini-projects may include poster systems, narrative explorations, typography studies, visual identity experiments, editorial layouts, or observational communication exercises.
Students begin integrating communication design with digital media, interaction systems, narrative environments, user experience, and information systems. They study:
Branding and identity systems
Typography and editorial systems
Interaction design fundamentals
Motion graphics and time-based media
Communication theory and semiotics
Information visualisation
Digital design tools and media systems
Students learn how communication increasingly operates across physical, digital, and interactive ecosystems.
Students transition into junior practitioners and enter their chosen specialisation pathway. Projects may involve:
Brand ecosystems and campaigns
Interactive communication systems
Animation and visual storytelling
Digital experiences and interfaces
Motion systems and immersive narratives
Public communication systems
UX and interaction ecosystems
Strategic communication platforms
Students also engage with advanced storytelling systems, digital production workflows, collaborative projects, and communication strategy.

Students complete advanced specialisation work, internships, cross-domain pathways, and a substantial capstone communication project. The final year emphasises strategic thinking, creative leadership, portfolio development, professional practice, systems thinking, and industry readiness.
Graduates leave with strong portfolios, research capability, production experience, interdisciplinary exposure, and readiness for advanced communication practice or postgraduate study.

Students choose one Within-Domain Specialisation (16–18 credits) starting in Year 3. These advanced tracks allow students to develop significant depth in a focused area of communication design while building on the strong conceptual, technical, and methodological foundation acquired in the programme core.
Visual Communication & Identity Systems Design

This specialisation focuses on creating comprehensive visual identity systems, branding architectures, packaging, editorial design, and environmental graphics. Students master advanced typography, layout systems, brand strategy, and multi-touchpoint visual storytelling. The track integrates design craft with strategic thinking to build cohesive, memorable, and culturally resonant communication systems that work across print, digital, and physical environments.
Career options include:
Brand Identity Designer
Visual Communication Designer
Packaging Designer
Editorial Designer
Environmental Graphics Designer
Corporate Identity Specialist

Graduates are prepared for roles in branding agencies, design consultancies, corporate in-house teams, publishing houses, and organisations that require strong, consistent visual systems and strategic brand communication. Graduates will work in brand consultancies, design studios, advertising agencies, media organisations, publishing, and digital communication ecosystems.
Animation and VFX Design

This specialisation develops expertise in character animation, motion graphics, visual effects, storytelling, and immersive animation techniques. Students learn advanced 2D/3D animation pipelines, compositing, narrative development, and production workflows for film, advertising, digital content, gaming, and emerging media platforms. The track combines artistic expression with technical proficiency to create compelling, emotionally engaging moving-image experiences.
Animation Designer
Motion Graphics Designer
VFX Artist
Character Animator
Storyboard Artist
Creative Director (Animation)

Graduates are equipped for careers in animation studios, VFX houses, advertising agencies, gaming companies, digital content platforms, and film & entertainment production. Graduates will have opportunities in animation studios, film production, gaming, OTT platforms, media companies, digital storytelling ecosystems, and immersive media industries.
Interaction Design

This specialisation prepares students to design intuitive digital experiences, user interfaces, micro-interactions, and interactive systems. Students explore UX/UI design, motion for interfaces, information architecture, user research, and prototyping of digital products across screens and emerging platforms. The track equips students to create seamless, engaging, and human-centred digital interactions that enhance user experience and communication effectiveness.
Interaction Designer
UX/UI Designer
Digital Experience Designer
Motion Designer for Interfaces
User Interface Specialist
Digital Product Designer

Graduates are prepared for roles in digital agencies, product companies, tech startups, web3 environments, and organisations focused on user-centred digital experiences and interactive communication. Graduates will find opportunities in technology companies, startups, digital product organisations, UX consultancies, consumer technology ecosystems, and interactive media industries.
Students choose one curated pathway (or design a custom one under faculty supervision):

Immersion is a central pillar of the B.Des. Communication Design programme at WPU GŌA. These carefully sequenced, credit-bearing experiences connect classroom learning with real-world contexts, enabling students to observe, research, create, test, and refine communication solutions across diverse cultural, social, industrial, and digital environments. They build cultural intelligence, user empathy, strategic insight, storytelling capability, and professional readiness essential for a communication designer.
The year begins with foundational immersions that help students transition from academic learning to reflective, human-centred communication practice.

Life Realisation Programme at Life Transformation Centre (LRP at LTC) (5+1 days, Pune & nearby village) develops self-awareness, empathy, ethical grounding, and holistic thinking, core qualities for any communication designer. Through direct engagement with nature, community, and reflective practices, students experience human stories, emotions, and values they will later interpret and communicate through design.

Pune Industry Immersion Experience (PIIX) Programme (4 days, Pune industrial belts) provides early exposure to corporate branding, visual communication in manufacturing, marketing systems, and real-world organisational storytelling.

International Learning & Engagement for Academic Development (I.L.E.A.D.) Programme (2 weeks, Europe) offers global perspectives on visual culture, contemporary design movements, digital media ecosystems, and cross-cultural communication through visits to leading design schools and creative innovation hubs.
Students connect core communication concepts with society, culture, media, and community realities.

Rural Engagement and Community Heritage (R.E.A.C.H.) Programme (5 days, Rural Goa) immerses students in rural life, allowing them to understand local narratives, traditional visual languages, communication needs, and grassroots storytelling through direct engagement and observation.

Asian Innovation & Industry Immersion (A.I.I.I.) Programme (1 week, Asian hubs) exposes students to global trends in branding, digital advertising, motion media, and emerging communication technologies at leading innovation forums and trade shows.

Social Ownership through Leadership, Innovation, and Development (SOLID) Internship (2 months, National/Regional) places students with NGOs, social enterprises, or development organisations, where they apply communication design skills to real social impact campaigns, awareness programmes, and community storytelling initiatives.
Students transition into junior communication designers through advanced scholarly and industry exposure.

Residency for Immersive Scholarly Engagement (RISE) Programme (2 weeks, IITs/IIMs/NITs) provides intensive academic immersion at India’s premier institutions, deepening exposure to advanced design research, digital communication strategies, and interdisciplinary media innovation.

Industry or Research Internship (3 months) offers extended hands-on experience in design studios, advertising agencies, digital media companies, branding firms, or content production houses, allowing students to work on live projects and build professional portfolios.
The final year focuses on synthesis and responsible creative leadership.

Student Active Journey in Administration & Governance (SAJAG) Programme (1-week, New Delhi) provides direct exposure to public policy, governance communication, national branding, and the role of design in shaping public discourse and civic engagement.

Signature Capstone / Startup Track (6 months) serves as the culminating experience. Students undertake a substantial brand system, transmedia campaign, information design project, or communication-driven social venture, integrating all prior academic and immersion learning into a high-impact, portfolio-ready deliverable.
Academic Requirement : Minimum 50% aggregate marks in Class 12 / 10+2 (or equivalent) from any recognised board (CBSE, ISC, State Boards, IB, Cambridge, etc.).
Compulsory Subject : English must be one of the subjects.
Stream : Open to students from any stream (Science, Commerce, Arts, or Vocational).
Portfolio : Submission of a portfolio showcasing creative work, design thinking, and visual communication skills is mandatory for all B.Des. candidates.
For International / NRI / OCI Candidates : Equivalent qualifications are accepted with AIU equivalence certificate where required.
The admissions committee evaluates students holistically, seeking applicants who demonstrate:
Creativity and curiosity
Visual thinking ability
Systems awareness
Initiative and originality
Communication capability
Reflective thinking
Empathy and social awareness
Potential for collaborative learning
For B.Des. applicants, portfolio submission is mandatory and forms an important part of the selection process.
WPU GŌA follows a holistic, multi-stage admission process designed to identify well-rounded, high-potential students who align with the university’s transdisciplinary and values-driven ethos.
Online Registration: Create an account on the WPU GŌA admissions portal and fill the registration form. Upload required documents, academic records, and a Written Essay (max 750 words).
Entrance Examination (for Indian applicants): Appear for the WPU GŌA Entrance Examination — a 60-minute online proctored test with MCQs in:
Logical Reasoning (30 marks)
Quantitative Ability (20 marks)
Verbal Ability (20 marks)
Computer Fundamentals (15 marks)
General Knowledge (15 marks). No negative marking.

Personal Interaction: Shortlisted candidates participate in a 20–30 minute online conversational interview with a faculty panel. The discussion focuses on your profile, motivations, thought process, and fit with WPU GŌA’s philosophy.
Holistic Evaluation: The Admissions Committee evaluates the complete profile using the ABC Model :
A gility & Empathic Responsiveness
B road-minded Orientation to Learning
C uriosity & Co-Creation for Change
Weightage is given to:
Academic performance
Entrance test score
Quality of Written Essay
Personal Interaction
Extracurricular achievements, leadership, and meaningful experiences
Merit List & Offer: Selected candidates receive a Provisional Offer Letter via email. They must accept the offer and pay the admission fee within the stipulated deadline to confirm their seat.
Document Verification & Final Admission: Submit original documents for verification. Upon successful verification, you receive your Unique Student ID and formal admission confirmation.
For precise dates, admission schedule, and the latest updates , please visit the official Admissions page and the detailed Admission Process & Schedule .
All seats are filled strictly on merit through the above process.
Merit-based and special scholarships are available for outstanding candidates.
A separate streamlined process applies (no entrance exam is required). Refer to the International Student Policy .
This is a special inaugural batch fee . The regular programme fee is ₹9,00,000 per year; the inaugural cohort receives a substantial subsidy.
Security Deposit : ₹50,000 (one-time, refundable at graduation or withdrawal, subject to conditions).
Full academic instruction, assessments, and examinations
Access to labs, studios, makerspaces, library (physical + digital), software licences, and learning management systems
Fully residential on-campus accommodation (triple-sharing)
Meal plan (7 days a week)
All mandatory immersions
IT infrastructure, high-speed internet, and basic medical facilities on campus
Security Deposit (₹50,000)
Books, study materials, stationery, and personal laptop
Winter/Summer term stay (if required)
Optional semester exchange programmes abroad
Additional certifications, advanced medical treatment, personal travel during vacations, or damage to university property
Hostel room upgrades (subject to availability and extra charge)

The stated fee covers the regular academic year (Monsoon + Spring semesters). Additional charges apply only if a student is required to stay on campus during winter or summer terms with prior permission.
Scholarships : Merit-based and special scholarships are available for deserving students. Scholarships apply primarily to tuition fees.
For the most accurate and latest fee details, payment schedule, and complete inclusions/exclusions, please visit the official Fee Structure page .

Q1. What makes the B.Des. Communication Design programme at WPU GŌA different from traditional design programmes? It combines visual craft with strategic thinking, research, behavioural insight, technology, and systems thinking. Students complete two specialisations (one Within-Domain and one Cross-Domain), engage in intensive studios every semester, undertake extensive immersions, and work on real-world communication projects, preparing them not just to create visuals, but to design meaningful, effective, and responsible communication systems.

Q2. Can I customise my degree? Yes. You choose one Within-Domain Specialisation and one Cross-Domain Specialisation. You can also design a custom Cross-Domain pathway under faculty guidance and select Directed Electives to further personalise your learning.
Q3. What Within-Domain Specialisations can I choose?

Q4. What is the Independent Inquiry Period (IIP)? A four-week, credit-bearing, self-directed period after Semester 5 where students pursue an independent design research project, product development exploration, or applied design challenge. It builds design independence and often informs the final Capstone.

Q5. How practical is the programme? Highly practical. Students learn through studio-based practice, build physical and digital prototypes, use professional digital media labs, engage with industry partners, and complete real client or social impact projects throughout the four years.

Q6. How do transdisciplinary studios work in this programme? Every semester includes at least one transdisciplinary studio, an intensive, project-based learning experience where you work in teams on real organisational challenges. Studios emphasise application, peer critique, iteration, and tangible deliverables (business plans, strategy decks, prototypes, etc.).

Q7. Is the programme suitable for students who want to become entrepreneurs? Absolutely. The Cross-Domain Specialisation options, combined with venture labs, incubation support through Centres of Excellence, and the option to convert your Capstone into a live communication or design venture, make the programme highly entrepreneurship-friendly.
Admission Process

Q8. What is the eligibility for B.Des. Communication Design? Minimum 50% aggregate in Class 12/10+2 (any stream) with English as a compulsory subject. Submission of a portfolio showcasing creative work and design thinking is mandatory.
Q9. Is a portfolio required? Yes. All B.Des. candidates must submit a portfolio that demonstrates their creative abilities, visual communication skills, and design thinking.
Q10. Can I apply if my Class 12 results are awaited? Yes. You can register with “Result Awaited” status and predicted scores. Final admission is confirmed after submission of the official marksheet.

Q11. What should I write in the Written Essay? Be authentic and reflective. Share your journey, why you want to study communication design, meaningful experiences, challenges overcome, and how your values align with WPU GŌA’s transdisciplinary and ethical philosophy. Focus on self-awareness and aspirations (max 750 words).
Q12. Is residential accommodation mandatory? Yes. WPU GŌA is a fully residential campus. Living in Learning Scholar Communities (LLSCs) is an integral part of the learning experience.
Fees & Scholarships
Q14. Are scholarships available? Yes. Merit-based and special scholarships are offered.
Immersions & Experiential Learning

Q15. How much real-world exposure will I get? You will complete approximately 11 months of structured professional experience through progressive immersions, a two-month social internship, a 12-week industry internship, and a full-semester Capstone.
Q16. Are all immersions mandatory? Yes. They form an integral part of the curriculum and are fully covered in the annual fee.

Q17. What types of internships are available during the programme? Students undertake a two-month social internship (SOLID) with NGOs or social enterprises in Year 2, a three-month professional internship in design studios, advertising agencies, or digital media companies in Year 3, and a full-semester Capstone project in Year 4. Together, these provide structured, progressive professional experience across diverse creative sectors.
Career & Future Prospects

Q18. What career options are available after B.Des. Communication Design? Graduates can pursue careers in branding, advertising, digital media, motion graphics, UX/UI, information design, social impact communication, publishing, content strategy, and creative direction. Many also prepare for higher studies at leading global institutions or launch their own design studios.
Q19. Can I pursue higher studies after this programme? Yes. The strong research focus, IIP, and Capstone make graduates highly competitive for postgraduate programmes in India and abroad.

Q20. What kind of placements and career support does WPU GŌA provide? Career readiness begins in Year 1. You build a strong Digital Portfolio, gain extensive industry exposure, and complete a high-impact Capstone. The university leverages the broader MIT-WPU network and industry partnerships for internships, projects, and final placements.

Q21. What is campus life like at WPU GŌA? WPU GŌA is a fully residential campus with Living Learning Scholar Communities (LLSCs) that enhance peer learning and personal growth. Students have access to studios, digital labs, makerspaces, sports facilities, and a vibrant creative community of peers from diverse backgrounds.
Q22. Are laptops mandatory? Yes. The programme is tech-enabled and requires students to bring their own laptop.
Q23. Whom should I contact for more information? Email: admissions@wpugoa.edu.in Helpline: +91 86050 07433
Q24. Does WPU GŌA accept SAT Scores? WPU GŌA accepts SAT Scores by College Board for admissions to all UG Programmes. Send us your scores directly, by using Scoresend Code: 77384.
How do we communicate meaning in a world overwhelmed by information, media noise, algorithms, and fragmented attention?
How can design shape narratives, build trust, influence behaviour, create accessibility, and help people navigate increasingly complex digital and physical environments?

And in a future shaped by artificial intelligence, immersive media, interactive systems, global connectivity, and rapidly evolving communication technologies, who will create the visual languages, stories, interfaces, and experiences that define how societies understand the world?
Students learn how communication operates across identities, institutions, public systems, brands, digital platforms, media ecosystems, and everyday human interactions.

Their industry work, research, and professional practice directly inform classroom learning, ensuring students understand not only how to create communication, but why communication shapes society.

Emerging media and immersive communication
Human-centred communication systems

Students select one specialisation track in Year 3 and complete advanced, domain-specific courses that provide significant depth. The specialisation tracks enable students to develop specialised expertise that prepares them for focused professional roles, entrepreneurship, or higher studies in their chosen area. The three available tracks are:
Directed Electives

These advanced elective courses offer students the flexibility to deepen their expertise within the chosen Within-Domain specialisation or to explore closely related topics that align with their academic and professional interests. Examples include Advanced Typography, Design Futures & Speculative Communication, Inclusive Communication Systems, Sustainable Media Systems, AI Tools for Designers, Public Policy Communication Design, Motion Design for Branding, Data Visualisation & Information Design, and Transmedia Storytelling Strategies.

The Directed Electives allow significant customisation, enabling students to tailor their degree according to their specific career aspirations — whether in brand identity systems, motion graphics, interaction design, information visualisation, social impact communication, or emerging interdisciplinary fields.
Cross-Domain Specialisation

In addition to their primary communication design specialisation, every student completes one Cross-Domain Specialisation from another academic cluster (Engineering, Humanities, or Business). Curated options include:

Students may also design a custom cross-domain pathway under faculty guidance. This additional pathway is a defining feature of WPU GŌA graduates and a critical requirement for solving complex, real-world problems. It fosters transdisciplinary fluency and inculcates the ability to integrate communication design insight with computational thinking, human behaviour understanding, or entrepreneurial frameworks — enabling graduates to address societal challenges at the intersection of visual storytelling, technology, innovation, and business.
Capstone / Signature Work

The culminating experience of the programme is a full-semester, 12-credit Capstone Project in Semester 8. Students undertake a substantial, real-world integrative project which may be a comprehensive brand identity system, transmedia storytelling campaign, information design solution for public systems, motion-driven narrative project, interactive digital experience, or social impact communication initiative. The Capstone is mentored by a faculty–industry panel and requires students to synthesise knowledge from their Within-Domain specialisation, Cross-Domain specialisation, extensive immersions, Independent Inquiry Period (IIP), and core communication design foundations into a meaningful, professional, and portfolio-ready contribution.
Experiential & Integrative Elements
Studio-Based Learning: At least one studio every semester — intensive, project-driven experiences focused on real organisational challenges.
Independent Inquiry Period (IIP): A four-week, credit-bearing, self-directed deep-dive after Semester 5.
Grand Challenge Studios & Transdisciplinary Projects: Collaborative work on university-wide societal challenges.
Continuous Digital Portfolio: A living record of projects, reflections, and achievements.
Mandatory Immersions: Global, national, rural, industry, trade shows, LRP at LTC, and Student Active Journey in Administration & Governance (SAJAG) Programme.
Programme Structure at a Glance
Year 1: Foundations of Visual Communication
Year 2: Communication Systems & Interaction
Year 3: Advanced Communication & Specialisation
Year 4: Professional Identity & Signature Work
Specialisations
Within-Domain Specialisations
1. Visual Communication & Identity Systems Design
Career options include: Brand Identity Designer, Visual Communication Designer, Packaging Designer, Editorial Designer, Environmental Graphics Designer, Corporate Identity Specialist.

Graduates are prepared for roles in branding agencies, design consultancies, corporate in-house teams, publishing houses, and organisations that require strong, consistent visual systems and strategic brand communication. They work across brand consultancies, design studios, advertising agencies, media organisations, publishing, and digital communication ecosystems.
2. Animation and VFX Design
Career options include: Animation Designer, Motion Graphics Designer, VFX Artist, Character Animator, Storyboard Artist, Creative Director (Animation).

Graduates are equipped for careers in animation studios, VFX houses, advertising agencies, gaming companies, digital content platforms, film & entertainment production, OTT platforms, digital storytelling ecosystems, and immersive media industries.
3. Interaction Design
Career options include: Interaction Designer, UX/UI Designer, Digital Experience Designer, Motion Designer for Interfaces, User Interface Specialist, Digital Product Designer.

Graduates are prepared for roles in digital agencies, product companies, tech startups, web3 environments, technology companies, UX consultancies, consumer technology ecosystems, and interactive media industries focused on user-centred digital experiences.
Cross-Domain Specialisations
Programme Immersions
Year 1: Foundational Cognitive and Ethical Grounding

Life Realisation Programme at Life Transformation Centre (LRP at LTC) (5+1 days, Pune & nearby village) develops self-awareness, empathy, ethical grounding, and holistic thinking — core qualities for any communication designer. Through direct engagement with nature, community, and reflective practices, students experience human stories, emotions, and values they will later interpret and communicate through design.
Year 2: Integration of Disciplines and Systems
Year 3: Professional Practice and National Networks

Residency for Immersive Scholarly Engagement (RISE) Programme (2 weeks, IITs/IIMs/NITs) provides intensive academic immersion at India's premier institutions, deepening exposure to advanced design research, digital communication strategies, and interdisciplinary media innovation.
Year 4: Leadership Synthesis and Impact

Student Active Journey in Administration & Governance (SAJAG) Programme (1 week, New Delhi) provides direct exposure to public policy, governance communication, national branding, and the role of design in shaping public discourse and civic engagement.
Eligibility and Admissions
Eligibility Criteria (B.Des. Communication Design)
Academic Requirement: Minimum 50% aggregate marks in Class 12 / 10+2 (or equivalent) from any recognised board (CBSE, ISC, State Boards, IB, Cambridge, etc.).
Compulsory Subject: English must be one of the subjects.
Stream: Open to students from any stream (Science, Commerce, Arts, or Vocational).
Portfolio: Submission of a portfolio showcasing creative work, design thinking, and visual communication skills is mandatory for all B.Des. candidates.
For International / NRI / OCI Candidates: Equivalent qualifications are accepted with AIU equivalence certificate where required.
What WPU GŌA Looks For
WPU GŌA follows a holistic, multi-stage admission process designed to identify well-rounded, high-potential students who align with the university's transdisciplinary and values-driven ethos.

Entrance Examination (for Indian applicants): A 60-minute online proctored test with MCQs in Logical Reasoning (30 marks), Quantitative Ability (20 marks), Verbal Ability (20 marks), Computer Fundamentals (15 marks), and General Knowledge (15 marks). No negative marking.

Personal Interaction: Shortlisted candidates participate in a 20–30 minute online conversational interview with a faculty panel focusing on profile, motivations, thought process, and fit with WPU GŌA's philosophy.

Holistic Evaluation (ABC Model): Agility & Empathic Responsiveness, Broad-minded Orientation to Learning, Curiosity & Co-Creation for Change. Weightage is given to academic performance, entrance test score, quality of essays, portfolio, personal interaction, and extracurricular achievements.
Merit List & Offer: Selected candidates receive a Provisional Offer Letter via email and must accept and pay the admission fee within the stipulated deadline to confirm their seat.

All seats are filled strictly on merit. Merit-based and special scholarships are available. A separate streamlined process applies for international students. Thanks to the Platform Strategy, students may switch programmes (subject to eligibility and seat availability) at the end of Year 1.
Fee Structure
Fee Break-up (per year)
Security Deposit: ₹50,000 (one-time, refundable at graduation or withdrawal, subject to conditions).
What is Included in the Annual Fee?
What is Not Included?
Scholarships: Merit-based and special scholarships are available for deserving students and apply primarily to tuition fees. Refund Policy as per the official Admissions Policy.
FAQs

It combines visual craft with strategic thinking, research, behavioural insight, technology, and systems thinking. Students complete two specialisations (one Within-Domain and one Cross-Domain), engage in intensive studios every semester, undertake extensive immersions, and work on real-world communication projects — preparing them not just to create visuals, but to design meaningful, effective, and responsible communication systems.

Studios emphasise application, peer critique, iteration, and tangible deliverables (business plans, strategy decks, prototypes, etc.).

title: B.Des in Integrated Product Design Course
description: Among leading Bachelor of Product Design colleges in India, WPU Goa’s B.Des in Integrated Product Design focuses on purpose driven, system level solutions.

card_image.alt: B.Des in Integrated Product Design Course

blocks.text: B.Des. in
Integrated Product Design

The B.Des. in Integrated Product Design at WPU GŌA prepares students to engage with these questions through a contemporary, studio-driven, and transdisciplinary design education grounded in systems thinking, sustainability, human experience, and real-world application.

At WPU GŌA, product design is approached not merely as form-making, but as a discipline that integrates technology, culture, materials, manufacturing, ecology, behaviour, communication, and systems thinking. Students learn to move fluidly between physical products, digital interfaces, service ecosystems, and complex social systems.

You will learn how people interact with products, spaces, technologies, and institutions, and how thoughtful design can improve experiences, create accessibility, enable sustainability, and solve complex societal problems.

The programme stands apart through its integration of design with technology, behavioural understanding, entrepreneurship, systems thinking, and sustainability. Through immersive studios, fabrication labs, prototyping workshops, field immersions, and collaborative projects, students develop the ability to design responsibly for an increasingly interconnected world.

You will be taught by faculty who are practising designers, researchers, technologists, strategists, and makers connected to WPU GŌA’s Centres of Excellence and interdisciplinary ecosystems. Their industry projects, research, and field practice directly shape the learning environment, ensuring students learn not only how to design, but why design matters.

The B.Des. in Integrated Product Design at WPU GŌA is a four-year undergraduate degree for students who aspire to design products, systems, services, and experiences that are human-centred, technologically aware, socially meaningful, and environmentally responsible.

The programme develops strong foundations across product design and systems thinking, human-centred design, visual communication and form exploration, ergonomics and human factors, materials and manufacturing processes, digital modelling and prototyping, and interaction design and smart products. Students learn design research and user understanding, and sustainability and circular systems. They become adept at service and systems innovation and design strategy and innovation management.

Students begin with common design foundations shared across all B.Des. programmes before progressively moving into integrated product systems, interaction environments, sustainable design thinking, and advanced specialisation pathways.

A defining strength of the programme is its dual-specialisation model. Every student develops depth through a Within-Domain Specialisation while also building interdisciplinary breadth through a Cross-Domain Specialisation. Graduates emerge prepared for careers across product innovation, interaction design, service systems, sustainable design, strategic design consulting, UX ecosystems, mobility systems, social innovation, entrepreneurship, and advanced design research.

Designers of Systems, Not Just Objects: Students learn to design products, services, infrastructures, interfaces, and ecosystems that function within larger social, technological, and environmental systems.

Integrated Physical–Digital Product Innovation: Students design products that combine form, electronics, interaction, software, and connected experiences preparing them for contemporary smart-product ecosystems.
Human-Centred & Systems-Level Design Thinking: The programme develops deep capability in user research, systems mapping, behavioural understanding, and design for complex real-world environments.

Goa as a Living Laboratory: Students engage with tourism systems, coastal ecologies, hospitality environments, craft ecosystems, urban growth, mobility systems, and sustainability challenges unique to Goa’s evolving landscape.

Ethical & Reflective Designers: Through the Life Realisation Programme at Life Transformation Centre (LRP at LTC), Student Active Journey in Administration & Governance (SAJAG) Programme, social immersion, and reflective practice, students develop empathy, responsibility, leadership, and ethical judgement.
From Prototype to Venture: Students can extend projects into entrepreneurship, incubation pathways, startup ecosystems, and innovation-led ventures supported by mentors and Centres of Excellence.

Transdisciplinary & Systems-Oriented Approach: Combines rigorous design education with engineering sensitivity, behavioural insight, business strategy, and sustainability science. Students learn to design not just objects, but complete product-service ecosystems that deliver meaningful value while addressing real societal and environmental challenges.

Dual Specialisation Model: One Within-Domain specialisation for deep design expertise and one Cross-Domain specialisation (from Engineering, Humanities, or Business) for versatile, future-ready capabilities, a defining feature that sets WPU GŌA graduates apart in the industry.

Intensive Studio-Based Learning: Project-driven studios every semester focused on real-world problems, from user research and concept development to advanced prototyping, testing, and deployment. This hands-on making culture builds both creative confidence and professional competence.

Strong Making & Prototyping Culture: Access to state-of-the-art makerspaces, digital fabrication labs (CNC, 3D printing), materials workshops, and prototyping facilities, enabling students to move rapidly from ideas to tangible, testable products.

Extensive Real-World Immersions: Global academic exposure, rural community engagement, Asian innovation trade shows, national residencies at premier institutions, industry internships, and civic governance programmes provide rich contextual understanding and industry readiness.

Human-Centred & Sustainable Focus: Deep emphasis on ethical design, circular economy principles, regenerative systems, inclusive innovation, and responsible technology, preparing graduates to create products that are desirable, feasible, viable, and planet-positive.

Entrepreneurial & Innovation Pathways: Strong support to convert studio projects into startups or ventures through incubation, Centres of Excellence, and mentorship, ideal for students who want to launch their own design-driven enterprises.

Leadership & Personal Development: Living Learning Scholar Communities (LLSCs), Life Realisation Programme at Life Transformation Centre (LRP at LTC), and wellness integration foster self-awareness, collaboration, resilience, and reflective practice essential for design leadership.

Future-Ready Career Preparation: Excellent placement potential and higher-study readiness in industrial design, mobility, smart systems, sustainable innovation, service design, UX for hardware, and emerging technology roles. Graduates are equipped to work in top design consultancies, corporations, startups, and social impact organisations.

Common Core: Every student at WPU GŌA, irrespective of their major, completes this carefully designed 40-credit University Common Core. It forms the intellectual bedrock of the entire academic ecosystem and develops ten essential graduate attributes. The Common Core equips students with transversal skills that transcend any single discipline. It creates a shared intellectual language across engineers, designers, psychologists, and managers so that graduates can collaborate effectively on complex, real-world problems. It ensures every student graduates with strong critical thinking, ethical reasoning, communication fluency, data literacy, and systemic perspective, capabilities that are increasingly valued in leadership roles.

Design Domain Foundation & Programme Core : Rigorous grounding in design fundamentals, form and aesthetics, materials and manufacturing processes, user-centred research, ergonomics, digital design tools, prototyping techniques, and design strategy. It progresses logically from Year 1 to Year 3, moving from foundational visual and making skills to advanced product systems thinking and complex project execution. The programme core builds strong disciplinary competence and functional mastery before students move into specialisations. This ensures graduates possess a solid design toolkit that is both conceptually deep and practically applicable.

Within-Domain Specialisation: Students select one specialisation track in Year 3 and complete advanced, domain-specific courses that provide significant depth. The specialisation tracks enable students to develop specialised expertise that prepares them for focused professional roles, entrepreneurship, or higher studies in their chosen area.
The three available tracks are:
(1) Service & Systems Design (2) Sustainable Design (3) Interaction Design

Directed Electives: These advanced elective courses offer students the flexibility to deepen their expertise within the chosen Within-Domain specialisation or to explore closely related topics that align with their academic and professional interests. Examples include Advanced Product Styling, Sustainable Packaging Systems, Craft Futures & Material Narratives, Speculative & Future Design, Design Research Methods (Advanced), Ergonomics for Special Needs, Smart Product Interfaces, and Circular Economy Strategies.

The Directed Electives allow significant customisation, enabling students to tailor their degree according to their specific career aspirations, whether in industrial design, mobility systems, sustainable product development, smart device innovation, service design, or emerging interdisciplinary fields.

Cross-Domain Specialisation: In addition to their primary product design specialisation, every student completes one Cross-Domain Specialisation from another academic cluster (Engineering, Design, or Business). Curated options include:
Computational Thinking & Creative Coding
Behavioural & Cognitive Psychology
Entrepreneurship in Creative Industries

Students may also design a custom cross-domain pathway under faculty guidance. This additional pathway is a defining feature of WPU GŌA graduates and a critical requirement for solving complex, real-world problems. It fosters transdisciplinary fluency and inculcates the ability to integrate design insight with computational thinking, human behaviour understanding, or entrepreneurial frameworks, enabling graduates to address societal challenges at the intersection of products, technology, innovation, and business.

Capstone / Signature Work: The culminating experience of the programme is a full-semester, 12-credit Capstone Project in Semester 8. Students undertake a substantial, real-world integrative project which may be an industry-sponsored product development assignment, sustainable product-service system, smart connected device, speculative future design concept, or social impact design venture. The Capstone is mentored by a faculty–industry panel and requires students to synthesise knowledge from their Within-Domain specialisation, Cross-Domain specialisation, extensive immersions, Independent Inquiry Period (IIP), and core design foundations into a meaningful, portfolio-ready contribution.

Experiential & Integrative Elements: To ensure every graduate has substantial real-world exposure, reflective practice, and the ability to translate theory into impactful action, learning at WPU GŌA extends far beyond classrooms through a rich ecosystem of experiential components:
Studio-Based Learning : At least one studio every semester — intensive, project-driven experiences focused on real organisational challenges.
Independent Inquiry Period (IIP) : A four-week, credit-bearing, self-directed deep-dive after Semester 5.
Grand Challenge Studios & Transdisciplinary Projects : Collaborative work on university-wide societal challenges.
Continuous Digital Portfolio : A living record of projects, reflections, and achievements.
Mandatory Immersion s : Global, national, rural, industry, trade shows, LRP at LTC, and Student Active Journey in Administration & Governance (SAJAG) Programme (detailed in the Immersions section).
Students begin by developing visual literacy, material sensitivity, design observation, systems thinking, and foundational making skills. They explore:
Drawing & visual communication
Form and composition
Design thinking and problem framing
Materials and manufacturing
Product form exploration
Design research fundamentals
Mini-projects may include observational studies, material explorations, product redesign exercises, ergonomic experiments, or rapid prototyping assignments.
Students begin integrating design with user behaviour, ergonomics, technology, prototyping, and systems understanding. They study:
Human-centred design
Ergonomics & human factors
CAD and digital modelling
Electronics for designers
Product-system integration
Interaction design fundamentals
Prototyping & testing
Students learn how products increasingly operate as part of larger physical-digital ecosystems.
Students transition into junior practitioners and enter their chosen specialisation pathway. Projects may involve:
Service-system innovation
Smart product ecosystems
Sustainable product systems
Circular design frameworks
Connected-device experiences
Product branding and strategy
Public systems design
Interactive environments
Students also engage with advanced fabrication systems, digital workflows, and innovation management.

Students complete advanced specialisation work, industry internships, cross-domain pathways, and a substantial capstone design project. The final year emphasises synthesis, leadership, portfolio development, strategic thinking, and professional maturity.
Graduates leave with strong portfolios, prototype systems, research capability, industry exposure, and readiness for contemporary design practice or postgraduate study.

Students choose one Within-Domain Specialisation starting in Year 3. These advanced tracks allow students to develop significant depth in a focused area of product design while building on the strong scientific, technical, and methodological foundation acquired in the programme core.
Service & Systems Design
Career options include:
Service Designer
Systems Designer
Design Researcher
Public Innovation Designer
Healthcare Systems Designer
Civic Design Consultant
Experience Strategist

Graduates are prepared for roles in design consultancies, government innovation labs, healthcare organisations, social enterprises, and large corporations seeking to redesign customer and citizen experiences. Graduates go on to work in consulting, healthcare, government innovation, NGOs, education systems, mission-driven organisations, and public infrastructure.
Sustainable Design
Sustainable Product Designer
Circular Economy Specialist
Environmental Design Strategist
Regenerative Design Consultant
Climate Resilience Designer
Eco-Design Lead
Graduates are equipped for careers in sustainable brands, green technology companies, circular economy startups, design consultancies, and organisations focused on climate-positive innovation.
Interaction Design
Digital Product Designer
UX Designer for Hardware
IoT Product Designer
Smart Device Experience Designer
Interaction Designer
Emerging Technology Designer

Graduates are prepared for roles in consumer electronics, mobility, health-tech, smart home systems, wearable technology companies, and innovation-driven organisations. Graduates go on to work in consumer technology companies, smart device firms, wearable technology brands, health-tech startups, mobility systems, and interactive product ecosystems.
Students choose one curated pathway (or design a custom one under faculty supervision):

Immersion is a central pillar of the B.Des. Integrated Product Design programme at WPU GŌA. These carefully sequenced, credit-bearing experiences connect classroom learning with real-world contexts, enabling students to observe, analyse, prototype, and refine design solutions across diverse human, industrial, and environmental settings. They build empathy, systems thinking, material intelligence, user insight, and professional readiness essential for a product designer.
The year begins with foundational immersions that help students transition from academic learning to reflective, human-centred design practice.

Life Realisation Programme at Life Transformation Centre (LRP at LTC) (5+1 days, Pune & nearby village) develops self-awareness, empathy, ethical grounding, and holistic thinking, core qualities for any designer. Through direct engagement with nature, physical work, and reflective practices, students experience the mind-body connection and human needs they will later address through design.

Pune Industry Immersion Experience (PIIX) Programme (4 days, Pune industrial belts) provides early exposure to manufacturing ecosystems, materials, production processes, and real-world product development environments.

International Learning & Engagement for Academic Development (I.L.E.A.D.) Programme (2 weeks, Europe) offers global perspectives on design innovation, sustainable systems, user culture, and contemporary product ecosystems through visits to leading design schools and innovation hubs.
Students connect core design concepts with society, culture, materials, and community realities.

Rural Engagement and Community Heritage (R.E.A.C.H.) Programme (5 days, Rural Goa) immerses students in rural life, allowing them to understand user needs, local materials, craft traditions, sustainability challenges, and grassroots innovation through direct observation and engagement.

Asian Innovation & Industry Immersion (A.I.I.I.) Programme (one week, Asian hubs) exposes students to global trends in product design, smart manufacturing, sustainable materials, and emerging technologies at leading innovation forums and trade shows.

Social Ownership through Leadership, Innovation, and Development (SOLID) Internship (2 months, National/Regional) places students with NGOs, social enterprises, or development organisations, where they apply design thinking to real social challenges such as affordable products, craft revival, or community systems.
Students transition into junior designers through advanced scholarly and industry exposure.

Residency for Immersive Scholarly Engagement (RISE) Programme (2 weeks, IITs/IIMs/NITs) provides intensive academic immersion at India’s premier institutions, deepening exposure to advanced design research, technology integration, and interdisciplinary innovation.

Industry or Research Internship (3 months) offers extended hands-on experience in design studios, manufacturing units, product development teams, or research labs, allowing students to apply theory, develop professional skills, and build portfolio work in real contexts.
The final year focuses on synthesis and responsible design leadership.

Student Active Journey in Administration & Governance (SAJAG) Programme (one week, New Delhi) provides direct exposure to public policy, governance, sustainable systems, and the role of design in shaping inclusive public infrastructure and policy.

Signature Capstone / Startup Track (6 months) serves as the culminating experience. Students undertake a substantial industry-sponsored product development project, sustainable system design, or design-driven startup initiative, integrating all prior academic and immersion learning into a high-impact, portfolio-ready deliverable.
Academic Requirement : Minimum 50% aggregate marks in Class 12 / 10+2 (or equivalent) from any recognised board (CBSE, ISC, State Boards, IB, Cambridge, etc.).
Compulsory Subject : English must be one of the subjects.
Stream : Open to students from any stream (Science, Commerce, Arts, or Vocational).
Portfolio : Submission of a portfolio showcasing creative work, design thinking, and visual communication skills is mandatory for all B.Des. candidates.
For International / NRI / OCI Candidates : Equivalent qualifications are accepted with AIU equivalence certificate where required.
The admissions committee evaluates students holistically, seeking applicants who demonstrate:
Creativity and curiosity
Visual thinking ability
Systems awareness
Initiative and originality
Communication capability
Reflective thinking
Empathy and social awareness
Potential for collaborative learning
For B.Des. applicants, portfolio submission is mandatory and forms an important part of the selection process.
WPU GŌA follows a holistic, multi-stage admission process designed to identify well-rounded, high-potential students who align with the university’s transdisciplinary and values-driven ethos.
Online Registration : Create an account on the WPU GŌA admissions portal and fill the registration form. Upload required documents, academic records, and a Written Essay (max 750 words).
Entrance Examination (for Indian applicants): Appear for the WPU GŌA Entrance Examination — a 60-minute online proctored test with MCQs in:

Quantitative Ability (20 marks)
Verbal Ability (20 marks)

General Knowledge (15 marks). No negative marking.

Personal Interaction: Shortlisted candidates participate in a 20–30 minute online conversational interview with a faculty panel. The discussion focuses on your profile, motivations, thought process, and fit with WPU GŌA’s philosophy.
Holistic Evaluation : The Admissions Committee evaluates the complete profile using the ABC Model :
A gility & Empathic Responsiveness
B road-minded Orientation to Learning
C uriosity & Co-Creation for Change
Weightage is given to:
Academic performance
Entrance test score
Quality of Written Essay
Personal Interaction
Extracurricular achievements, leadership, and meaningful experiences
Merit List & Offer : Selected candidates receive a Provisional Offer Letter via email. They must accept the offer and pay the admission fee within the stipulated deadline to confirm their seat.
Document Verification & Final Admission: Submit original documents for verification. Upon successful verification, you receive your Unique Student ID and formal admission confirmation.
For precise dates, admission schedule, and the latest updates , please visit the official Admissions page and the detailed Admission Process & Schedule .
All seats are filled strictly on merit through the above process.
Merit-based and special scholarships are available for outstanding candidates.
A separate streamlined process applies (no entrance exam is required). Refer to the International Student Policy .
This is a special inaugural batch fee . The regular programme fee is ₹9,00,000 per year; the inaugural cohort receives a substantial subsidy.
