title: Researach Institutes: Ocean sciences and Management
description: Explore ocean sciences and management research at WPU Goa focusing on marine ecosystems, sustainability, and environmental resilience.

blocks.text: Our Research Themes and Areas of Focus
Our Research Areas
Areas and Focus
Coral Reef and Mangrove Ecology
Deep-Sea Ecosystems
Sustainable Aquaculture
Ocean Governance and Protection
Microplastic Pollution
Impact Areas
From Research to Real Solutions
Coral Reef Protection and Restoration
Protecting coral reefs from thermal stress, supporting reef-dependent communities.
Sustainable Fisheries and Food Security
Blue Economy Equity and Development
Ocean Pollution Mitigation
Marine Biodiversity Conservation
Protecting critical marine habitats through research, latest autonomous technology and AI analytics.
Ocean Governance and Justice
Coastal Resilience and Adaptation
Ocean Health Monitoring
Research Methodologies
Research That Listens, Learns, and Transforms
We conducting systematic studies of ocean species, ecosystems, and evolving ocean conditions.
Developing and deploying advanced instruments, sensors, and AI-enabled systems to support ocean research.
Assessing how coastal communities derive, share, and benefit from ocean-based livelihoods and resources.
Evaluating legal frameworks and institutional structures to strengthen ocean governance and management.
Collaborating with local and Indigenous communities to integrate their knowledge, priorities, and practices.
Drawing expertise from science, engineering, economics, law, and cultural studies to address complex ocean challenges..
We Implementing and assessing innovative approaches through real-world ocean trials and demonstration sites.
Be a Part of WPU GŌA
Members
Prof. Gyana Ranjan Tripathy

Associate Professor, Department of Earth and Climate Science, IISER Pune Prof. Gyana Ranjan Tripathy is an Associate Professor in the Department of Earth and Climate Science at IISER Pune, where he leads the GRASP (Geochemical Research for Aquatic and Sedimentary Processes) laboratory. His group primarily aims to quantify low-temperature aquatic processes, including chemical weathering and climate interactions in the Himalayan river system, marine sediment geochemistry, and the global carbon cycle. His research expertise spans weathering, geochronology, and isotope geochemistry. He was selected as a Young Associate of the Indian Academy of Sciences in 2015, recognised for his work on surficial weathering processes, Re-Os geochronology, reconstruction of marine redox conditions of the geological past, and chemical weathering of Indian river basins. He has published extensively in leading international journals including Chemical Geology and Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta.
Prof. Sunil Kumar Singh

Director, CSIR - National Institute of Oceanography, Goa Prof. Sunil Kumar Singh is the Director of CSIR – National Institute of Oceanography (CSIR-NIO), Goa, and a Professor at the Physical Research Laboratory, Ahmedabad. Born in 1971, he completed his B.Sc. and M.Sc. (Tech.) from Banaras Hindu University and his PhD in Geology from the Physical Research Laboratory and M.S. University, Vadodara. He is a leading Indian geochemist known for his studies in low-temperature elemental and isotope geochemistry, with research that has advanced understanding of the evolution of the Himalayas and biogeochemical cycling in the Indian Ocean. His research interests span geochemistry, isotope chemistry, palaeoclimate, palaeoceanography, nutrient cycling, and biogeochemistry. He has been conferred the Lifetime Achievement Award of the Indian Society of Applied Geochemists (ISAG) for his contributions to Earth Sciences and applied geochemistry.

title: Advanced Materials & Energy Systems Research
description: Discover advanced materials and manufacturing research at WPU Goa focused on energy systems, innovation, and next-generation technologies.

blocks.text: Rationale
Growing demand for advanced materials in aerospace, transportation, electronics, sensing, and healthcare, as well as in strategic sectors such as agriculture, defence, aerospace industries.
Need for sustainable, affordable, green, and energy-efficient materials manufacturing processes.
India’s strategic focus on strengthening materials manufacturing capabilities under initiatives like Make in India and Atmanirbhar Bharat.
Opportunity to position WPU GŌA as an international leader in advanced materials research. Innovation, IP creation, translation to industrial sector.
Bridging the gap between academic research and industrial applications in materials research.
Objectives
Advance transdisciplinary research in advanced materials, manufacturing and emergent device systems.
Design and develop new materials and affordable green processing methodologies for multiple industrial and societal applications.
Foster industry-academia-policy convergence for translational impact.
Train next-generation researchers and engineers through experimential learning and hands-on training.
Build global partnerships to strengthen India’s role in advanced manufacturing and emergent device development ecosystems.
Mandate

The Institute will advance research and innovation in advanced materials and manufacturing technologies as well as emergent device systems in multiple sectors of national importance and international significance such as semiconductors, quantum, advanced sensors, robotics, AI/ML etc. By integrating science, engineering, and industry collaboration. Its mandate is to develop sustainable materials, green, smart and energy efficient manufacturing, and translational research leading to IP creation (patents), high impact publications, and proof-of-concept demonstrations (TRL 4) that could invite industry interest.
Opportunity to position MIT-WPU Goa as an international leader in advanced materials research. Innovation, IP creation, translation to industrial sector.
Research Themes
Advanced Functional Materials
Sustainable Manufacturing
Smart Manufacturing and Industry 4.0
Plan
Phase I (Year 1–2)
Establish core labs, recruit faculty, initiate pilot projects.
Phase II (Year 3–5)
Expand research clusters, launch doctoral programs, secure industry partnerships.
Phase III (Year 5+)
Scale translational research, incubate startups, achieve global recognition.
Impact and Outcomes

Development of new materials, affordable and green manufacturing technologies as well as new architectures of emergent devices for energy, environment, transportation, robotics and other such sectors.addressing, industrial and societal challenges.
Creation of startups and patents driving India’s manufacturing competitiveness.
Enhanced student employability and global competitiveness.
Strengthened academic-industry-policy linkages.
Contribution to Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).
Partnerships and Alliances
Collaborations with international universities and research centres in materials and manufacturing.
Alliances with industry leaders in aerospace, automotive, electronics, healthcare and strategic sectors such as defence, agriculture, aerospace, water etc.
Engagement with government agencies and policy think-tanks.
Partnerships with NGOs and community organizations for societal outreach.
Infrastructure and Resources
State-of-the-art laboratories for materials synthesis, characterization, and manufacturing.
Computational facilities for materials modelling and simulation.
Innovation hubs and incubator spacesfor startups.
Collaborative classrooms and seminar halls.
Access to shared resources across MIT-WPU campuses.
Be a Part of WPU GŌA
Members
Prof. Neil Robertson

Professor, School of Chemistry, The University of Edinburgh Prof. Neil Robertson is a Professor in the School of Chemistry at the University of Edinburgh, where he leads the Robertson Research Group. He graduated with a BSc and PhD from the University of Edinburgh and carried out post-doctoral research at the Freie Universität Berlin and the University of Wales, Bangor. His research focuses on the synthesis, characterisation, and application of new materials for energy conversion and storage, including lithium-ion, lithium-sulphur, sodium-ion, and zinc-ion battery electrode materials, supercapacitor and pseudocapacitor electrodes, and dye-sensitised and perovskite solar cells. He currently serves as Dean of International Partnerships for the College of Science & Engineering at the University of Edinburgh, and as President Elect of the Materials Chemistry Community of the Royal Society of Chemistry. He has collaborated with several industrial partners on battery and solar energy technologies.
Prof. Ashutosh Sharma

Institute Chair Professor, Department of Chemical Engineering, IIT Kanpur Prof. Ashutosh Sharma is an Institute Chair Professor in the Department of Chemical Engineering at IIT Kanpur, and elected President of the Indian National Science Academy (2023–25). He served as Secretary to the Government of India, Department of Science and Technology, from 2015 to 2021, where he initiated landmark programs in quantum technologies, clean energy, innovation, and international scientific collaborations. He has published over 350 peer-reviewed papers with over 20,000 citations, filed 15 patents, and is the founding coordinator of the Nanosciences Centre and Advanced Imaging Centre at IIT Kanpur. His numerous honours include the Padma Shri, the inaugural Infosys Prize in Engineering and Computer Science, the TWAS Science Prize, the Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Prize, and the UNESCO Medal for Contribution to Development of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology. He is a Fellow of the Indian National Science Academy, Indian Academy of Sciences, and The World Academy of Sciences (TWAS).
Prof. Dipankar Das Sarma

Honorary Professor, Solid State and Structural Chemistry Unit (SSCU), IISc Bangalore Prof. Dipankar Das Sarma (popularly known as D.D. Sarma) is an Honorary Professor at the Solid State and Structural Chemistry Unit (SSCU), IISc Bangalore, where he spent over three decades as a faculty member. He obtained his Integrated MSc in Physics from IIT Kanpur (1977) and his PhD from IISc Bangalore (1982), and is a world leader in three distinct research areas: strongly correlated electron systems, semiconductor nanoparticles, and energy materials. His honours include the Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Prize (1994), TWAS Prize in Physics (2007), the UNESCO Biennial Javed Husain Prize, and the Italian Government's Knight of "The Order of the Star of Italy" (2014). He has garnered over 22,900 citations with an h-index of 78, and serves as Senior Editor of ACS Energy Letters. He is a Fellow of all four major Indian science academies.

title: Research Institute on Biodesign and Biotechnology
description: Explore bio-design and biotechnology research at WPU Goa advancing health, sustainability, and innovation through interdisciplinary science.

blocks.text: The Research Institute on Biodesign and Biotechnology at WPU GŌA campus is envisioned as a pioneering hub for transdisciplinary education, cutting-edge research, and innovation.
About Institute
Vision
Aims and Objectives
Advance transdisciplinary research in advanced materials, manufacturing and emergent device systems.
Design and develop new materials and affordable green processing methodologies for multiple industrial and societal applications.
Foster industry-academia-policy convergence for translational impact.
Train next-generation researchers and engineers through experimential learning and hands-on training. .
Build global partnerships to strengthen India’s role in advanced manufacturing and emergent device development ecosystems
Mandate

To establish MIT-WPU Goa as a global leader in biodesign and biotechnology research, education, and innovation, driving viable solutions for healthcare, biotechnology, environment, and agriculture through transdisciplinary collaboration and societal impact.
Research Themes
Biodesign for Healthcare
Synthetic Biology and Bioengineering
Environmental Biotechnology
Computational Biology and AI
Agricultural Biotechnology
Activities
Establish research labs and innovation hubs.
Conduct doctoral and postdoctoral programs.
Organize conferences, workshops, and hackathons.
Launch student innovation challenges in biodesign.
Facilitate patent filing, technology transfer, and incubation.
Plan
Phase I (Year 1–2)
Establish core labs, recruit faculty, initiate pilot projects.
Phase II (Year 3–5)
Expand research clusters, launch doctoral programs, secure industry partnerships.
Phase III (Year 5+)
Scale translational research, incubate startups, achieve global recognition.
Impact and Outcomes
Development of innovative biotechnologies addressing healthcare, environment, and agriculture.
Creation of startups and patents driving India’s bioeconomy.
Enhanced student employability and global competitiveness.
Strengthened academic-industry-policy linkages.
Contribution to sustainable development goals (SDGs).
Partnerships and Alliances
Collaborations with international universities.
Alliances with industry leaders in biotech, pharma, and medtech.
Engagement with government agencies and policy think-tanks.
Partnerships with NGOs and community organizations for societal outreach.
Infrastructure and Resources
State-of-the-art laboratories for materials synthesis, characterization, and manufacturing.
Computational facilities for materials modelling and simulation.
Innovation hub and incubator space for startups.
Collaborative classrooms and seminar halls.
Access to shared resources across MIT-WPU campuses.
Be a Part of WPU GŌA
Members
Prof. Sanjay Balkrishna Maggirwar
Prof. Rabah Boukherroub

Research Director, CNRS, and Professor, Institute of Electronics, Microelectronics & Nanotechnology, University of Lille, France Prof. Rabah Boukherroub is a Research Director at CNRS and Group Leader at the Institute of Electronics, Microelectronics and Nanotechnology (IEMN), University of Lille, France. He received his PhD in Chemistry from the University of Paul Sabatier, Toulouse, and his research interests span nanostructured functional materials, surface chemistry, and photophysics of semiconductor and metal nanostructures, with emphasis on biosensors, nanomedicine, photocatalysis, and energy-related applications. He is a co-author of over 440 research publications and 31 book chapters on nanotechnology, materials chemistry, and biosensors, and holds 11 patents. He serves as Associate Editor of ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces and as Co-editor in Chief of AI & Materials. His global collaborations span China, India, the Netherlands, and beyond.

Be a Part of WPU GŌA
Members
Prof. Siba Kumar Udgata

Professor, School of Computer & Information Sciences, University of Hyderabad Prof. Siba Kumar Udgata is a Professor at the School of Computer and Information Sciences, University of Hyderabad, and heads the Centre for Modelling, Simulation and Design (CMSD), a high-performance computing facility at the university. He holds a Master’s and PhD in Computer Science with specialisation in mobile computing and wireless communication, and his primary research interests span wireless communication, wireless sensor networks, IoT, intelligent algorithms, and soft computing. He was a United Nations Fellow and worked as a research fellow at the United Nations University / International Institute for Software Technology (UNU/IIST), Macau in 2001. He serves as Principal Investigator on multiple Government of India funded research projects focused on sensor network applications and swarm intelligence techniques, and has over 2,700 citations with more than 161 publications to his credit.

title: Research Institutes: Environmental Sustainability & Resilience
description: Discover environmental sustainability research at WPU Goa addressing climate change, ecosystems, and resilience through interdisciplinary innovation.

blocks.text: Our Research Themes and Areas of Focus
Our Research Areas
Areas and Focus
Climate Adaptation and Impacts
Adaptation and Resilience
Renewable Energy Systems
Industrial Decarbonisation
Nature-Based Solutions
Impact Areas
From Research to Real Solutions
Climate-Resilient communities
Energy Access and Equity
Green Job Creation and Just Transition
Sustainable Food and Agriculture
Industrial Transformation
Natural Capital and Biodiversity
Research Methodologies
Research That Listens, Learns, and Transforms

In this institution, we work together with a variety of stakeholders to understand the impact of- and ways to address responsible integration of AI in human life. Through transdisciplinary methods, stakeholders are brought together in dialogue to talk about challenges and opportunities for sustainable solutions in various fields, including health, mental wellbeing, education, farming, or social justice.
Climate science and data analytics including downscaled projections and impact modelling
Transdisciplinary collaboration across engineering, ecology, economics, and social science
Policy and governance research supporting evidence-based regulation and institutional reform
Pilot projects and adaptive management testing and refining interventions in real-world contexts
Community-engaged research ensuring strategies address lived experience and local priorities
Nature-based and technological solutions that work synergistically rather than in isolation
Lifecycle and systems analysis examining trade-offs and co-benefits across environmental, social, and economic dimensions
Be a Part of WPU GŌA
Members
Prof. Aditya D. Mohite

Professor, Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, and Department of Materials Science & Nano Engineering, Rice University Prof. Aditya D. Mohite is a Professor in the Departments of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering and Materials Science & NanoEngineering at Rice University, Houston, and Director of REINVENTS (Rice Engineering INitiatiVe for ENergy Transition and Sustainability). He earned his B.S. and M.S. in physics and solid-state physics from Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda, India, and his PhD in Electrical Engineering from the University of Louisville, before serving as a Staff Scientist at Los Alamos National Laboratory. His research focuses on understanding structure-function properties in materials to control charge and energy transport across interfaces, with applications in next-generation photovoltaics, photocatalysis, and decarbonisation technologies. He has published over 180 peer-reviewed papers in journals including Science, Nature, and Nature Materials, garnering over 25,000 citations, and has been named to the Clarivate Highly Cited Researchers list five consecutive years from 2019 to 2023.
Prof. Amartya Mukhopadhyay

Professor, Department of Metallurgical Engineering & Materials Science, IIT Bombay Prof. Amartya Mukhopadhyay is a Professor in the Department of Metallurgical Engineering & Materials Science at IIT Bombay, and a globally recognised pioneer in sodium-ion (Na-ion) battery technology. He holds a B.E. in Metallurgical Engineering from NIT Durgapur, an M.Tech from IIT Kanpur, and a DPhil in Materials from the University of Oxford, UK, followed by post-doctoral research at Brown University, USA. He was invited by the journal Science to contribute a perspective article in his area of research, published as Li metal battery, heal thyself (Science, 2018). In December 2024, he was honoured with the Tata Transformation Prize in Sustainability, selected from 169 entries across 18 Indian states, for his groundbreaking work in developing safe, affordable, and sustainable Na-ion batteries. He is also a recipient of the SwarnaJayanti Fellowship 2020–21 and the INAE Young Engineer Award 2016.
Prof. Pradeep Thalappil

Institute Professor, Department of Chemistry, IIT Madras Prof. Pradeep Thalappil is an Institute Professor and Deepak Parekh Institute Chair Professor in the Department of Chemistry at IIT Madras, the highest academic distinction at the institute. He earned his PhD in chemical physics from the Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, under Professors C.N.R. Rao and M.S. Hegde, followed by post-doctoral fellowships at UC Berkeley and Purdue University. He is an author of over 550 scientific papers and an inventor of over 100 patents, and has co-founded seven companies translating nanotechnology into affordable water purification solutions that have reached over 10 million people. He was elected a Foreign Member of the US National Academy of Engineering in 2024 for his contributions to cluster chemistry and affordable drinking water solutions. His accolades include the Padma Shri, Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Prize, ENI Award, and VinFuture Prize.

Selection Process at WPU GŌA

At WPU GŌA, selection is based on your overall profile using the ABC Model, not a single score. We consider entrance test performance, academics, essays, personal interactions, and extracurricular or professional achievements.
Assessment Framework
The internal assessment process holistically evaluates the following components :
Candidate’s Profile
Past Academic Records
Entrance Examination
Written Essay
Online Personal Interaction
Extracurricular Activities and/ or Professional Achievements
ABC Model of Selection
WPU GŌA places great importance on three interconnected dimensions (ABC)
Agility & Empathic Responsiveness, which is defined by the ability to adapt to change, show resilience, and demonstrate empathy
Broad-Minded Learning Orientation & Self-Development, which is demonstrated by aspects such as curiosity-driven learning, independent thinking, and openness to growth.
Curiosity for Complex Problems & Co-Creation for Change, which is exemplified by engagement with real-world issues through collaboration and teamwork
WPU GŌA Entrance Exam
The WPU GŌA Entrance Exam is a 100-marks, 60-minutes online multiple-choice assessment. Exam structure & weightage as follows
Written Essay

WPU GŌA employs a holistic review process for potential students, focusing on more than just standardized test scores. The written essay allows candidates to highlight their individuality, goals, and values.
Video Essay
In addition to the written essay, candidates are required to submit a short video (2-3 minutes) in which they introduce themselves and explain why the committee should select them.
The Video Essays are to be submitted within the registration form as a link to an UNLISTED YouTube video in the provided space.
Personal Interaction

All candidates who submit the registration form will be invited to participate in an online Personal Interaction as part of the admission process. The Personal Interaction duration is 20 to 30 minutes.
It will include a discussion based on your overall profile.
Explore and Experience Learning in Goa
Student Experiences
Participate in immersive student experience programs crafted to shape your skills and mindset, giving directions to your ideas and thoughts.
Educational Model

title: Student Experience
description: Discover student experience programmes at WPU Goa including global immersions, industry exposure, and hands-on learning beyond classrooms.

blocks.text: Your Experience Journey at WPU GŌA
Professional Counselling
Professional Counselling and Coaching

Students receive personalised professional coaching that helps them reflect on how their academic background, skills, and experiences align with future education and career pathways. Coaches work closely with students to clarify their values, strengths, and aspirations, enabling them to build a strong personal and professional profile that can guide future work and study decisions.
Alumni-Led Coaching
Alumni-Led Corporate Coaching

MITWPU alumni play a vital role in student development by offering mentorship grounded in real-world experience. Alumni-led coaching provides students with insights into current industry trends, market expectations, and emerging opportunities. Through these interactions, students learn how to position themselves effectively and take confident next steps in their professional journeys.
Experiential Learning Cell
End-to-end support for logistics, partnerships, planning & monitoring

The Learning Cell functions as a dedicated academic and wellbeing support unit focused on enhancing student success. It offers structured assistance such as academic skill-building workshops, learning strategy support, time-management guidance, and stress-management resources.

The Learning Cell also identifies students who may need additional support and works collaboratively with faculty and mentors to ensure timely interventions, fostering a supportive and inclusive learning environment.
Career readiness, Business and leadership skills, Professional networking, Opportunity navigation
End-to-end support for logistics, partnerships, planning & monitoring
Partner identification and program coordination
Calendar creation and communication
Student support and logistics
Monitoring, assessment, and reporting
Explore and Experience Learning in Goa
University Philosophy

Learning at WPU GŌA is deeply field-based. Students engage with real-world challenges, restoring farmlands, designing zero-waste systems, and modelling carbon-neutral mobility at local, national, and global scales. Knowledge is built through practice, reflection, and impact.
At WPU GŌA, campus life blends learning, creativity, sports, and culture, fostering growth, friendships, and exploration beyond the classroom.
Be Part of WPU GŌA
Please fill out the form, and our team will get in touch with you!
Frequently Asked Questions
Experiential Learning & Immersions

WPU GŌA believes “the world is your classroom.” Rather than confining learning to campus, students undertake a sequence of immersions in diverse settings. In Year 1, students go on a Global Immersion to international innovation hubs, visiting universities, companies, design labs, and cultural centres to build global awareness. They also do a National Immersion (typically a fortnight) at premier Indian institutes (like IITs, IIMs, national labs) to learn from top faculty and connect with national research networks. From Year 2 on, students have Industry Immersions: programme-aligned workplace visits, internships, and workshops in industry settings, plus participation in international trade expos. Social and Rural Immersions place students in communities, NGOs, or social enterprises, where they conduct field projects on sustainability, social innovation, and grassroots problems. A dedicated Life Transformation Centre (LTC) immersion, a 5-day programme in nature focuses on personal growth (physical fitness, leadership, values, etc.). These experiences are cumulative: in the final year, each student completes a Capstone Project (often industry- or research-based) that integrates all prior learning and solutions developed across these contexts. Together, these layers of immersion ensure students learn in the world, not just about it.
WPU GŌA is building a network of partners in innovation hubs (like Singapore, Silicon Valley, and Europe). Specific partner lists are updated annually based on the Global Immersion schedule.

Our focus is on “Career Readiness” rather than just a placement day. Through the Digital Portfolio and Assistantship programmes, students would get an opportunity to be “pre-placed” or scouted by industry partners during their Year 3 “Grand Challenges.”

The journey begins with a global immersion programme that exposes students to international innovation hubs, design labs, and cultural ecosystems to build cultural intelligence. In the third year, students can choose to spend some time internationally studying at one of our partner universities on a voluntary basis.
Yes. With India’s growing startup economy, the education programmes emphasize disruptive thinking and innovation, supported by specialized hubs called Centres of Excellence (CoEs).
Absolutely. Our Centres of Excellence (CoEs) act as incubators, providing legal, design, and business strategy support for students who wish to launch their ventures before graduation.

Immersions are structured, mandatory learning experiences that take students beyond the classroom into real-world ecosystems—global, national, industry, social, and personal. They are a core academic requirement, not add-ons, and are designed to deepen learning through lived experience.
All core immersions are mandatory and form part of the academic credit structure. Optional semester-long international exchanges may be available in later years.

Yes. Every immersion—especially industry, trade show, and capstone experiences is mapped to programme learning outcomes. Students do not “travel to observe”; they travel to learn, analyse, and produce outcomes linked to their discipline.

Yes. All mandatory domestic and international immersions—including travel, accommodation, meals, local transport, and insurance—are included in the annual fee. For immersions starting at the onset of a semester the travel cost to the venue of immersion is to be borne by the parents. Consequently, for immersions ending with the semester the travel cost from the venue of immersion to place of journey will be borne by the parents.

Immersions expose students early to how careers function. They help students refine interests, build professional networks, develop confidence in unfamiliar environments, and create portfolio-ready work well before graduation.

Because optional experiences privilege confidence, resources, or chance. Mandatory immersions ensure every student regardless of background develops global awareness, real-world problem-solving ability, and professional maturity as part of the academic design.
Each immersion is mapped to:
Pre-immersion coursework and research questions
On-ground academic assignments, observations, or field studies
Post-immersion reflection, assessment, and portfolio documentation
Students are evaluated on learning outcomes, not participation alone.
Students engage with:
Universities and research labs (academic exposure)
Companies, studios, and innovation hubs (applied learning)
Cultural institutions and cities (contextual understanding)
They analyse how different societies approach innovation, governance, design, and enterprise—linked back to coursework.
India itself contains vastly different knowledge ecosystems. Exposure to IITs, IIMs, national labs, and public institutions allow students to:
Experience academic rigor at the highest national level
Build peer networks beyond their home university
Understand how large-scale national systems function
This immersion builds confidence and aspiration early.
Industry immersions are progressive:
Early years focus on observation, systems understanding, and problem framing
Later years involve internships, deliverables, and ownership
Students are treated as learners embedded in real systems.
Because leadership without empathy is incomplete. These immersions help students understand:
Human behaviour under constraints
Grassroots innovation and resilience
Ethical dimensions of decision-making
This is critical for careers in policy, business, technology, and design.

LTC addresses dimensions traditional universities ignore self-awareness, emotional maturity, discipline, and values. It ensures students grow as humans, not just professionals—reducing burnout and identity confusion later in life.
The capstone integrates:
Global exposure
National systems understanding
Industry practice
Social context
Students do not start capstones from theory alone; they start from lived experience across ecosystems.
Each student completes eight high-impact experiential requirements, including:
Five immersions (global, national, social/rural, industry, LTC)
A social organization internship and an industry internship
A capstone or industry project
These are sequenced to increase in complexity and responsibility year-on-year.
Each immersion generates structured outputs such as:
Reflection portfolios
Field notes and system maps
Stakeholder analyses
Problem briefs that feed into later studios or capstones
These are assessed and archived in the student’s academic portfolio.
Life Transformation Centre (LTC)

LTC was created to address a growing gap in modern education: students graduating with technical competence but lacking emotional grounding, ethical clarity, resilience, and a sense of purpose. It responds to rising stress, identity confusion, burnout, and disengagement among young adults.
Unlike short-term motivational programmes, LTC is:
Experiential, not instructional
Reflective, not performative
Process-oriented, not outcome-chasing
There are no lectures on “success formulas” or competitive exercises. Growth emerges through lived experience, structured reflection, and guided silence.
Responsibility before authority
Service before status

Self-regulation before influencing others Students learn that leadership begins with managing oneself, emotions, ego, discipline, and intent. How are students supported emotionally during the LTC experience? The programme is facilitated by trained mentors and counsellors. Activities are carefully sequenced to be introspective but safe. Students are never forced to disclose personal matters, and emotional boundaries are respected at all times. How does LTC impact students after they return to campus? Students typically return with:
Greater emotional steadiness
Improved self-discipline and focus
Stronger peer bonds

Clearer personal values These outcomes influence how students handle academic pressure, teamwork, leadership roles, and ethical dilemmas in later years. How does LTC connect to students’ long-term careers? Employers increasingly value resilience, judgment, empathy, and ethical decision-making. LTC builds these foundational traits, enabling graduates to sustain high performance, handle ambiguity, and lead responsibly over long careers. Why is LTC mandatory rather than optional? If optional, such experiences often attract only already-reflective students. Making LTC mandatory ensures every student, regardless of background or personality, has the opportunity for pause, introspection, and grounding during formative years. How does LTC align with global higher-education best practices? Globally, leading institutions are reintroducing wellbeing, ethics, and reflection into education. LTC places WPU GŌA ahead of this curve by institutionalising inner development rather than treating it as an add-on. What long-term habits does LTC aim to instill? LTC aims to instill habits of:
Reflection before reaction
Discipline over impulse
Balance between ambition and wellbeing

Responsibility toward self, society, and environment These habits often remain with students long after graduation. What activities are built into the Life Realisation Programme? LTC’s Life Realisation Programme rests on five interconnected pillars, each designed to awaken different dimensions of a student’s personality. These pillars are
Agriculture & Nature
Physical Fitness & Sports
Team Building & Leadership
Patriotism & Nation Building

Spirituality & Peace Why are these five activities combined into a single Life Realisation Programme? Because human development is multidimensional. Agriculture grounds humility, fitness builds resilience, teamwork develops leadership, patriotism instils responsibility, and spirituality provides inner balance. Together, they shape students into grounded, disciplined, and purposeful individuals not just degree holders. Why does LTC include agriculture-based activities? Agriculture reconnects students with effort, patience, and natural cycles. By working with soil, crops, and living systems, students develop humility, gratitude, and respect for resources—qualities often missing in digitally mediated, outcome-driven lives. What do students do during the agriculture and nature module? Students participate in activities such as sowing, harvesting, caring for plants and animals, observing biodiversity, and learning about water conservation and sustainable practices. These are hands-on, guided experiences, not theoretical lessons. How does working with nature contribute to personal development? Nature slows students down. It teaches delayed gratification, responsibility, and acceptance of uncertainty. Students learn that not all outcomes are controllable—an essential mindset for leadership, innovation, and life balance. Why is physical fitness treated as a core activity at LTC? Physical vitality is foundational to mental clarity, emotional balance, and resilience. LTC treats fitness not as competition, but as a lifelong discipline that supports sustained performance in academics and careers. What kind of physical activities are included? Activities include traditional Indian games, outdoor challenges, endurance tasks, team sports, and movement-based exercises. The focus is on participation, stamina, coordination, and self-awareness—not athletic excellence. What kind of leadership is cultivated through the team-building activities? LTC promotes leadership rooted in empathy, accountability, and service. Students learn that leadership is not authority or dominance, but the ability to listen, collaborate, and take responsibility for collective outcomes. What activities help develop these leadership qualities? Students engage in group challenges, problem-solving tasks, leadership treks, and collaborative assignments that require trust, communication, and mutual reliance. Roles rotate, ensuring everyone experiences both leadership and followership. How does this prepare students for real-world leadership? Real leadership involves ambiguity, interpersonal complexity, and ethical choices. LTC places students in controlled but challenging situations where these qualities are practised experientially rather than taught theoretically. What does “patriotism” mean in the context of LTC? At LTC, patriotism is an inner commitment to contributing responsibly to society and the nation. It is not symbolic or political, but rooted in awareness, duty, and respect for India’s civilisational and cultural heritage. How is patriotism experienced during the programme? Students engage with India’s history, philosophy, arts, and national service ideals. Interactions with defence personnel, discipline-based activities, and discussions on civic responsibility foster respect, courage, and national consciousness. How does this activity influence students beyond campus life? Students develop a deeper sense of social responsibility and ethical citizenship. This influences how they approach careers, leadership roles, and decisions that affect communities and institutions. What does spirituality mean at LTC? Spirituality at LTC is experiential and secular. It focuses on self-awareness, inner calm, and clarity of thought rather than belief systems, rituals, or religious instruction. What practices are included in the spirituality and peace module? Students are introduced to meditation, yoga, breathwork, silence, reflection, and contemplative practices inspired by India’s wisdom traditions. These help students cultivate presence, emotional balance, and mental discipline. How does this benefit students in a demanding academic environment? These practices help students manage stress, regulate emotions, and maintain focus. Over time, they support better decision-making, reduced anxiety, and a healthier relationship with ambition and achievement. Active Citizenship Programme What is the Active Citizenship Programme (ACP)?

The Active Citizenship Programme is a structured, short-term academic immersion in Delhi designed to help students understand how a constitutional democracy functions in practice through exposure to institutions, public systems, and civic processes rather than political advocacy. Many graduates are professionally skilled but civically disengaged. ACP addresses this gap by helping students understand rights, duties, governance mechanisms, and the role of informed citizens in sustaining democracy.

Delhi is India’s governance and policy nerve centre. It hosts Parliament, constitutional bodies, ministries, courts, think tanks, media institutions, and civil society organisations, making it the most appropriate environment for observing democratic systems in action.

No. The programme is strictly non-partisan and non-political. It does not promote any ideology, party, or political viewpoint. The focus is on constitutional literacy, civic responsibility, and institutional understanding.
Students participate in:
Structured institutional visits
Policy and governance briefings
Guided observation of civic processes
Discussions with practitioners (bureaucrats, policy professionals, journalists, legal experts, civil society leaders)
All activities are educational and moderated.

Social service focuses on helping communities. Active Citizenship focuses on understanding systems. ACP helps students grasp how laws, policies, institutions, and public accountability mechanisms shape societal outcomes.

Regardless of the major such as engineering, management, design, media, or humanities students benefit by understanding how policy, regulation, public institutions, and civic frameworks intersect with professional practice.

Leadership in complex societies requires institutional literacy, ethical judgment, and civic awareness. ACP equips students to engage responsibly with public systems rather than remain passive or cynical observers.
Yes. The programme is mandatory because civic understanding is considered a core graduate attribute, not an optional interest.
The programme aligns with NEP 2020’s emphasis on:
Constitutional values
Ethical citizenship
Holistic education
Social responsibility
Classroom courses explain what institutions are. ACP shows students how institutions function through real-world exposure, observation, and guided interpretation.
No. The aim is not opinion formation, but capacity building helping students think critically, independently, and responsibly about public life.
LTC focuses on inner discipline and values. ACP focuses on external systems and civic responsibility. Together, they shape grounded individuals who are both self-regulated and socially conscious.
Graduates develop:
Constitutional awareness
Civic confidence
Ethical public reasoning
A sense of responsibility toward institutions and society
These qualities influence careers in business, technology, public service, entrepreneurship, and leadership.

In an era of misinformation, polarisation, and disengagement, democracies require citizens who understand systems, respect institutions, and act responsibly. ACP ensures graduates are not only skilled professionals, but informed citizens.

title: University Philosophy
description: Discover WPU Goa’s philosophy focused on transdisciplinary education, experiential learning, and holistic development for future-ready global leaders.

blocks.text: Our Transdisciplinary Approach

The greatest problems facing humanity, such as climate change, migration or health disparity, cannot be solved by specialists working in isolation. We need professionals who are trained in their own disciplinary field and are able to collaborate across differences. This requires a fundamentally different education and research.
What Is Transdisciplinary Education?

Transdisciplinarity is the guiding academic and research philosophy of WPU GŌA. It reshapes how knowledge is created, taught, and applied by integrating insights from multiple disciplines with the lived experience and expertise of practitioners, communities, industry, and policymakers. Transdisciplinary Education begins with real-world problems, not academic silos, and works to generate knowledge that is meaningful, actionable, and socially relevant. Traditional universities often isolate knowledge within disciplinary boundaries, making collaboration difficult. Today’s global and local challenges such as climate crises, public health inequities, urban crowding, and technological disruption are too complex to be understood or solved by any single field.
Imagine Any Problem Today
Traditional Disciplines All Ask Their Own Questions
An Engineer Might Ask: How can we design better water systems?’
An Environmentalist Might Ask: How can we ensure water does not get polluted?’
A Public Health Expert Might Ask: How do we reduce negative consequences of polluted water for families and children?’
Lack of clean water for villages
Transdisciplinary Education Combines These and Asks
How is the problem experienced and viewed by various stakeholders?
What are the systematic relationships between causes and consequences?
What opportunities are available to evoke change?
Solution driven, not discipline focused
Integrative across boundaries
Collaborative with stakeholders
Action-oriented toward transformation
Transdisciplinary education starts with real problems and integrates whatever knowledge solves them.
Solution driven; not discipline focused
The Ecosystem for Human Development

WPU GŌA has been envisioned not as a conventional university but as a living ecosystem of learning, discovery, wellness, and purpose, a place where students grow not only intellectually, but physically, emotionally, socially, and ethically. The five pillars of this ecosystem work in continuous dialogue with each other, forming a seamless developmental arc for every learner.

This ecosystem is deeply aligned with the institutional philosophy articulated across your documents: education that is transdisciplinary, discovery-driven, experiential, and human-centered, rooted in wellbeing, societal purpose, and the cultivation of leadership for the 21st century.
Pedagogy: Learning by Doing

Learning at WPU GŌA is deeply field-based. Students engage with real-world challenges, restoring farmlands, designing zero-waste systems, and modelling carbon-neutral mobility at local, national, and global scales. Knowledge is built through practice, reflection, and impact.
Our Core Educational Pillars
The Academic Core

Our Centres of Excellence strengthen the university’s capacity to explore relevant themes, including spirituality, agriculture, governance, leadership, and more, creating hubs of advanced knowledge and innovation.
College of Human Performance and Longevity
Living Learning Scholar Communities
Transdisciplinarity as our core Philosophy
Explore and Experience Learning in Goa
Student Experiences
Participate in immersive student experience programme crafted to shape your skills and mindset; giving directions to your ideas and thoughts.
Know More
At WPU GŌA, campus life blends learning, creativity, sports, and culture, fostering growth, friendships, and exploration beyond the classroom.
Be a Part of WPU GŌA
Frequently Asked Questions
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG)

The SDGs provide a global framework for addressing complex, interconnected challenges such as climate change, inequality, health, and sustainable growth. WPU GŌA’s transdisciplinary curriculum, experiential immersions, research institutes, and Grand Challenge Studios are designed around solving precisely such real-world, system-level problems, making the SDGs a natural reference framework for learning.
WPU GŌA’s model aligns particularly with:

• SDG 3 – Good Health & Wellbeing (College of Human Performance & Longevity, LTC) • SDG 4 – Quality Education (Transdisciplinary, experiential, research-integrated model) • SDG 8 – Decent Work & Economic Growth (Industry immersions, entrepreneurship focus) • SDG 9 – Industry, Innovation & Infrastructure (Centres of Excellence, AI literacy) • SDG 11 – Sustainable Cities & Communities (Urban, social, and systems projects) • SDG 12 – Responsible Consumption & Production (Net-zero campus, conscious dining) • SDG 13 – Climate Action (Ecology-focused immersions and projects) • SDG 16 – Peace, Justice & Strong Institutions (Active Citizenship Programme)

Many Grand Challenges, capstones, and immersion assignments are framed around themes such as sustainability, inclusion, public systems, ethical technology, and social innovation — all of which map directly to SDG priorities.

Global employers, research institutions, and governments increasingly frame innovation, policy, and funding priorities around SDGs. Familiarity with these frameworks helps students position their work in globally relevant contexts.

title: Prof. Walter Leal Filho
description: Vice Chancellor

blocks.text: A Message from the

"Welcome to the World Peace University (WPU) GŌA - a visionary institution established to redefine the landscape of higher education. Our campus is not just a place of learning; it is a sanctuary for holistic development, where academic rigour meets the tranquility of nature."

By breaking down traditional silos between subjects, we empower students to think critically, innovate freely, and develop a comprehensive understanding of the world-emerging as both experts and versatile thinkers.
Three Pillars of Excellence
Transdisciplinary Learning
India's first university to fully embrace a transdisciplinary model, dissolving subject silos so students engage with the world's complexity as it truly is interconnected and multifaceted.
Sustainability at the Core
Sustainability guides our curriculum, operations, and campus design. Students don't just learn about environmental stewardship-they live it, on a campus that acts as a living laboratory.
Holistic Development
Fully residential and community-driven, WPU GŌA creates spaces for collaboration, creativity, and cultural engagement because education extends far beyond the classroom walls.
Prof. Walter Leal
Vice Chancellor, WPU GŌA Top 1% Cited Researcher Globally
CHAIR POSITIONS
Hamburg University of Applied Sciences, Manchester Metropolitan University, Uppsala University, Merito University (Poland)
FIELD EXPERIENCE
70+ countries; missions for EU, World Bank, OECD, UNESCO, UNEP
RESEARCH FOCUS
Sustainable Development Climate Change
A Scholar of Global Standing
Academic Qualifications
RESEARCH AREA 1
Founder of the European School of Sustainability Science and Research. Editor-in-Chief of the Encyclopedia of the UN Sustainable Development Goals – 17 volumes, 1,700+ chapters, 2,300+ authors.
RESEARCH AREA 2
IPCC Contributing Author (AR5 & AR6). Founder of the International Climate Change Research and Information Programme (ICCIRP). Editor of the leading peer-reviewed Climate Management book series.
Academic & Research Leadership
Founding of IUSDRP & European Sustainability Networks
Established the Inter-University Sustainable Development Research Programme and the European-North American Sustainability Research Consortium, creating global collaborative infrastructure.
International Journal of Sustainability in Higher Education (IJSHE)
Founding Editor since 2000-the world’s only journal dedicated exclusively to sustainability in higher education contexts.
IPCC Contributions - AR5 & AR6
Served as Review Editor for the 5th Assessment Report and Lead/Contributing Author for the landmark 6th Assessment Report on climate science.
Teaching & Doctoral Supervision
"A university that doesn't just prepare you for a career but for a life of purpose, impact, and meaning in a rapidly changing world."
Begin Your Journey
at WPU GŌA

title: WPU GŌA Open House 2026
description: The WPU GŌA Open House , held on 20 June 2026, marked an important milestone in the university’s outreach efforts, engaging prospective students, parents, educators, and academic counsellors across more than 20 cities. The event served as a platform to introduce the vision, academic philosophy, and…

blocks.text: 20 June 2026

The WPU GŌA Open House , held on 20 June 2026, marked an important milestone in the university’s outreach efforts, engaging prospective students, parents, educators, and academic counsellors across more than 20 cities. The event served as a platform to introduce the vision, academic philosophy, and distinctive learning environment of WPU GŌA while addressing queries related to higher education and career opportunities.

The sessions highlighted the university’s student-centric approach, immersive learning ecosystem, industry engagement, global exposure opportunities, and emphasis on holistic development. The university’s senior leadership interacted with attendees, providing insights into WPU GŌA’s academic framework and the wide range of opportunities available to students.

Participants also received detailed information about the university’s undergraduate programmes, including B.Tech. in Computer Science and Engineering, Bachelor of Management Studies (Hons.), B.Des. in Integrated Product Design, B.Des. in Communication Design, and B.Sc. (Hons.) in Psychology. The interactive format encouraged meaningful discussions between the senior leadership, students, and parents, enabling them to better understand the university’s offerings and campus experience.

The successful completion of the multi-city Open House reflects WPU GŌA’s commitment to engaging with aspiring learners and their families while providing a comprehensive overview of its academic ecosystem and educational opportunities.
Date
Venue
GŌA India
In The Media

MIT World Peace University launches WPU GŌA

title: WPU GŌA Signs MoU with Makers Asylum Foundation to Advance Innovation and Maker-Led Learning
description: WPU GŌA has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Makers Asylum Foundation that opens new avenues of learning in the fields of innovation, entrepreneurship, design and maker-led

blocks.text: WPU GŌA has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Makers Asylum Foundation that opens new avenues of learning in the fields of innovation, entrepreneurship, design and maker-led education.

The collaboration brings together WPU GŌA’s academic ecosystem and Makers Asylum’s hands-on maker culture to help students move beyond classroom learning and engage with real-world problem-solving. This association will enable students and faculty to explore workshops, programmes, events, mentoring, guest sessions and knowledge-sharing initiatives that promote experimentation, creativity and practical application.

The partnership will also enable academic exchange and unlock opportunities for collaborative programs, grants, fellowships, and institutional partnerships through government, philanthropies, corporate and other sources. The MoU seeks to build access to such opportunities to strengthen the link between ideas, skills, and meaningful impact.

The collaboration gives students a chance to learn by doing, build with purpose, work across disciplines and develop an entrepreneurial mindset. It also reflects WPU GŌA’s continued commitment to fostering an environment that encourages innovation, nurtures collaboration and empowers young learners to shape future solutions.
The MoU was signed on 30th June 2026 in Goa, another step to experience-led, future-ready learning at WPU GŌA and strengthening its focus on student outcomes and innovation-led education.

title: Leadership That Delivers Conclave with BNI Goa Region
description: Read key insights from a one-day conclave focused on effective leadership, collaboration, and organizational success.

hero_image.alt: Leadership That Delivers Conclave with BNI Goa Region

blocks.text: WPU Goa × BNI Goa Region
One-Day Leadership Conclave

The WPU Goa One-Day Leadership Conclave on “Leadership That Delivers” marked a landmark moment in the institutional journey of WPU Goa campus. Co-organised in partnership with BNI Goa Region, the conclave convened over 250 participants for an immersive evening of world-class leadership dialogue, institutional recognition, and community investment.

The gathering brought together industry leaders, corporate professionals, MSME owners, startup founders, entrepreneurs, and academics — united by a shared conviction that leadership has never been more consequential than it is today.

Conceptualised under the institutional vision of Dr. Rahul V. Karad, Executive President, WPU Goa, and convened by Dr. Urvashi Makkar, Dean of the Ramcharan School of Leadership, MIT-WPU Pune, the conclave established WPU Goa as a genuine hub of leadership thought and professional community in Goa.

“WPU Goa is being built not merely as a place of education, but as an ecosystem that connects leadership, industry, recognition, and transformation. Every event like this conclave is a demonstration — not just a promise — of that vision.”
— Dr. Rahul V. Karad, Executive President, WPU Goa
FEATURED SPEAKERS
Voices That Shaped the Evening
Session: Leadership Deep-Dive · 4:30 PM – 6:00 PM (90 Minutes)

One of the most sought-after management advisors in the world, Prof. Ram Charan has worked for over five decades with CEOs and boards of globally iconic organisations including GE, Bank of America, DuPont, Novartis, Dell, and Honeywell. A prolific author of 30+ books — including Execution: The Discipline of Getting Things Done (with Larry Bossidy) — he holds a doctorate from Harvard Business School where he was a Baker Scholar.

His exclusive 90-minute Leadership Deep-Dive anchored the conclave in the principles of exponential organisational growth, customer obsession, long-term market leadership, and the irreplaceable value of people as the primary competitive asset.

“The most important resource any organisation has is not capital, not technology, not even its customers. It is the quality of its leadership at every level. And leadership quality is a function of deliberate, sustained, and honest investment in people.”
— Prof. Ram Charan
Session: Lead to Grow: Leadership for MSMEs · 6:00 – 6:20 PM

With four decades of hands-on experience in India’s premier automotive manufacturing sector, Mr. Kaul led transformative initiatives at the ANAND Group — one of India’s most respected automotive components manufacturers with combined revenues exceeding INR 10,000 crore. His session delivered practitioner-grounded insights covering cash flow discipline, governance as competitive advantage, quality culture (“Office Quality”), and the 4W1H innovation framework.

“Leadership is not about having all the answers. It is about creating belief when results are not yet visible. It is about building systems that outlast individuals. Most importantly, it is about transforming mindset before transforming business.”
— Mr. Sunil Kaul
Session: Leadership for Sustainable Growth · 6:20 – 6:40 PM

As steward of one of India’s most enduring food brands since 1956, Mr. Malhotra spoke from personal conviction and multi-generational business experience about what sustainable leadership truly means. His session presented a model in which ethical values — integrity, trustworthiness, care, and commitment — sit at the intersection of value delivered to employees, customers, and investors. He illustrated environmental sustainability through Weikfield’s solar-powered Bakori mushroom plant, which generates 17 lakh units of power annually and reduces carbon emissions by 1,400 tons per year.
“Economic Value + Ethical Values + Environmental Sustainability = Sustainable Profits. This is not idealism. This is the only business model that works across generations.”
— Mr. Ashwini Malhotra
KEY LEARNINGS
Leadership Wisdom From the Conclave

The three sessions together generated a rich, multilayered body of leadership wisdom. Despite distinct backgrounds and areas of focus, the three speakers converged on deeply shared convictions about what leadership truly demands.
On Customer & Stakeholder Centricity
Customer obsession is not a function or a team — it is an organisational philosophy that must govern every decision and every hire. (Prof. Ram Charan)
True economic value is created when businesses genuinely serve all stakeholders — not just shareholders. (Mr. Ashwini Malhotra)
Goodwill from customers, employees, and communities is the most durable competitive asset any organisation can build. (Mr. Ashwini Malhotra)
On Talent & People Development
Talent is the ultimate competitive advantage. The bar must be raised continuously and deliberately. (Prof. Ram Charan)
Building systems that outlast individuals is the highest form of leadership achievement. (Mr. Sunil Kaul)
Transforming mindset is the precondition for transforming business. Invest in both. (Mr. Sunil Kaul)
On Governance, Ethics & Trust
Governance is not compliance — it is the practical foundation of trust, and trust is the foundation of every sustainable business relationship. (Mr. Sunil Kaul)
Ethical values are not constraints on economic growth — they are its most powerful and most durable enablers. (Mr. Ashwini Malhotra)
Social responsibility must be unconditional. The moment it becomes transactional, it loses both its moral and business value. (Mr. Ashwini Malhotra)
On Innovation & Long-Term Thinking
Early failure is better than late failure. Create structures that honour, study, and celebrate failures as learning vehicles. (Mr. Sunil Kaul)
Innovation requires structure: the 4W1H framework (What, Why, When, Where, How) converts challenges into opportunities. (Mr. Sunil Kaul)
Long-term market leadership, not quarterly profit, is the north star of enduring strategy. (Prof. Ram Charan)
Environmental sustainability and economic profitability are allies when approached with long-term thinking and genuine commitment. (Mr. Ashwini Malhotra)
INAUGURAL AWARDS CEREMONY
WPU Goa Leadership Excellence Awards 2026

Twenty-five distinguished leaders from Goa’s corporate, startup, MSME, and sustainability sectors were felicitated across four award categories — celebrating leadership that delivers at every scale, in every sector, with integrity and impact.
COMMUNITY INVESTMENT
Merit Scholarships Announced

Among the most emotionally resonant moments of the evening, WPU Goa announced merit-based scholarships for 8 outstanding students from the 10+2 level. Recipients receive cash awards of ₹30,000 and ₹10,000 with fee support across three years of their graduation programme.

Recipients were selected on the basis of meritorious academic performance with consideration for marks obtained, board context, and association with the WPU Goa campus ecosystem. The scholarship announcement was met with warm, sustained applause from all 250+ participants.
STRATEGIC ROADMAP
What This Conclave Sets in Motion

The Leadership Conclave was not a one-time event — it was the first visible expression of WPU Goa’s long-term commitment to building a leadership ecosystem in Goa. Dr. Rahul V. Karad has outlined an ambitious roadmap for the years ahead.
Annual Leadership Conclave
To be institutionalised as a flagship annual event, growing in scale, speaker quality, and community impact year on year — becoming a marquee fixture in Goa’s professional calendar.
MSME & Startup Support Ecosystem
Developing incubation, mentoring, and capacity-building programmes for Goa’s entrepreneurial community, leveraging the expertise, networks, and resources of WPU Goa.
Academic-Industry Co-Design
Working with industry partners to co-design curriculum, research projects, and learning experiences ensuring WPU Goa graduates are immediately relevant and effective in the professional world.
Scholarship & Talent Pipeline
Expanding the merit-based scholarship programme annually, with a goal of doubling recipients by 2027–28 and creating structured pathways from scholarship to employment.
WPU Goa Leadership Network
Formal establishment of a structured network comprising inaugural awardees, future cohorts, conclave speakers, and institutional partners — with quarterly engagement platforms.
Research & Policy Engagement
Developing research programmes in partnership with government, industry, and civil society that address the specific development challenges and opportunities of Goa.

ABC Model of Selection
Guidance for Writing an Effective Essay
What was the challenge, and why was it significant?
How did they respond? What actions did they take?
What did they learn about themselves or others through this experience?
How has this shaped their ability to handle uncertainty or complexity?
What skills would they like to develop further to become more adaptable and empathetic?
What activities have they engaged in? (sports, arts, projects, hobbies, entrepreneurial ventures, special interests, etc.)
Why did they choose to invest time in these areas?
How did these experiences broaden their perspective or challenge their assumptions?
What new questions, interests, or insights emerged?
How do they plan to continue or deepen this learning journey?
Were they part of an initiative? (volunteering, sustainability efforts, social justice work, community organizations, candidate advocacy, etc.)
What drew them to this cause?
What makes this cause complex or challenging to address?
What did they learn from working with others, especially those with different perspectives or backgrounds?
How do they envision contributing to similar efforts in the future?
The written essay is a separate section in the registration form, and it should be written in English

The 750-word limit must be strictly adhered to. Shorter essays are permitted, but exceeding this limit will result in the essay not being evaluated, with the consequence of potentially not getting admitted to WPU GŌA
The written essay should be authentic and express the writer's genuine voice, perspectives, and lived experiences
It should reflect deeply on their journey, focusing on self-awareness and personal growth rather than listing achievements

It should be written honestly without exaggeration or falsification. There are no right or wrong answers; what matters is clarity of thought and how what is meaningful to them is conveyed to the committee
